proposals.title: Correlating RT-PCR Positivity with Viral Culture Viability in Asymptomatic Cases: A Public Health Investigation in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: RT-PCR is the gold standard for diagnosing viral infections, but its detection of viral RNA does not necessarily indicate the presence of transmissible, replication-competent virus. This distinction is critical for public health policy, as isolating asymptomatic individuals based solely on PCR positivity has significant social and economic costs. Recent work on tecovirimat resistance in monkeypox and HCV outbreaks in Los Angeles clinics underscores the need to accurately define infectiousness to guide containment measures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of asymptomatic individuals who test positive for specific viral pathogens via RT-PCR will not harbor culturable, transmissible virus, and that this proportion is associated with specific viral load thresholds and host immune factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using existing public health data and residual clinical specimens from Los Angeles County. Cases will be asymptomatic individuals who tested positive for viruses of interest (e.g., MPXV, HCV) via RT-PCR during defined outbreak periods (2022-2025). We will attempt viral culture on these PCR-positive specimens using standard cell lines (e.g., Vero E6 for MPXV). Viral load (Ct value) and host demographic/clinical data will be extracted. Statistical analysis will use logistic regression models to identify factors (e.g., Ct value, time since exposure) associated with culture positivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical, culture-based evidence to refine the clinical and public health interpretation of RT-PCR results. It will contribute data-driven thresholds (e.g., Ct values) that better correlate with transmissibility risk. The findings will directly inform public health guidelines in Los Angeles County and similar jurisdictions on isolation protocols for asymptomatic cases, optimizing resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While the concept of PCR vs. culture discordance is known, this study applies it directly to contemporary public health case management of outbreaks (MPXV, HCV) in a major metropolitan area, linking laboratory findings with real-world containment policies. It leverages Dr. Terashita's established methods of analyzing county-associated public health data to answer a fundamental diagnostic question.

proposals.title: A Public Health Implementation Study: Validating a Rapid Antigen Test-Based Isolation Protocol for High-Risk Respiratory Virus Outbreaks in Los Angeles County Clinical Settings
proposals.motivation: During infectious disease outbreaks, reliance on RT-PCR for case confirmation creates critical delays in isolation, fueling transmission, as seen in the 2022 mpox outbreak. This is especially problematic in high-risk clinical settings like pain clinics, where vulnerable populations congregate. A rapid, definitive isolation trigger is needed for immediate public health action.
proposals.hypothesis: For specific respiratory viruses (e.g., influenza, RSV) during defined outbreak periods, a positive rapid antigen test result has a sufficiently high positive predictive value (PPV) to serve as a definitive, standalone criterion for immediate isolation, without requiring confirmatory RT-PCR.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective diagnostic accuracy study during the 2025-2026 respiratory virus season in Los Angeles County. Partnering with the L.A. County Department of Public Health, we will recruit participants with symptoms from sentinel outpatient clinics. All participants will receive both a point-of-care antigen test and a RT-PCR test. Using pre-2026 statistical models, we will calculate the condition-specific PPV of antigen tests, incorporating local, real-time prevalence data. We will then design and simulate a protocol where a positive antigen test triggers immediate isolation, evaluating its impact on transmission reduction versus a standard PCR-confirmation pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, operational framework for using antigen tests as 'rule-in' tools for rapid isolation during outbreaks, directly informing L.A. County public health policy. It will generate evidence for a paradigm shift from confirmation to immediate action, potentially reducing the effective reproductive number (Rt) in outbreak settings.
proposals.novelty: While antigen tests are widely used for 'ruling out' infection, their formal validation and deployment as high-confidence 'rule-in' tools for mandatory isolation is a novel public health strategy. This inverts their standard purpose, prioritizing outbreak containment over diagnostic certainty for the individual.

proposals.title: Validation of Industrial Hygiene Air and Surface Sampling Methods for the Detection of SARS-CoV-2 Antigen in Clinical and Public Settings in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, environmental surveillance for SARS-CoV-2 remains critical for understanding transmission risks in congregate settings, yet methods are often complex and lab-dependent. Industrial hygiene offers standardized, field-deployable techniques for aerosol and surface contaminant sampling, but their adaptation for direct viral antigen detection is underexplored. This gap limits real-time, on-site risk assessment capabilities for public health departments, such as those in Los Angeles County, which has a history of investigating outbreaks in clinics and other facilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established industrial hygiene sampling methods, specifically bioaerosol impingers and surface wipes, can be effectively paired with rapid antigen detection tests (RADTs) to provide a sensitive and practical system for the environmental monitoring of SARS-CoV-2.
proposals.method: We will adapt two core industrial methods: (1) Liquid-based bioaerosol sampling using SKC BioSamplers in rooms of known COVID-19 cases, and (2) standardized surface sampling using pre-moistened macrofoam wipes on high-touch areas. Sampled eluates will be concentrated via centrifugation and analyzed using commercially available, FDA-authorized SARS-CoV-2 RADTs. The protocol will be validated against gold-standard RT-PCR of parallel air and surface samples. Statistical analysis, including calculation of sensitivity, specificity, and limits of detection, will be performed using R, with spatial mapping of positive samples in ArcGIS to identify transmission hotspots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, low-cost protocol for environmental antigen surveillance that public health teams can deploy during outbreak investigations without immediate laboratory processing. It will generate actionable data on SARS-CoV-2 contamination patterns in real-world settings, directly informing infection control measures. The project will also establish a methodological bridge between industrial hygiene and public health microbiology for future pathogen surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While environmental SARS-CoV-2 detection via PCR is common, the direct application of industrial hygiene form factors and workflows to enable on-site antigen detection is novel. This approach bypasses the need for laboratory nucleic acid extraction and amplification, offering a potential paradigm shift towards rapid environmental risk assessment.

proposals.title: Bayesian Sequential Testing for Enhanced Outbreak Surveillance: A Framework Applied to Mpox and Other Pathogens in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Public health surveillance in Los Angeles County faces challenges in rapidly and accurately interpreting sequential diagnostic test results during outbreaks, as seen with mpox and other pathogens. Current practice often treats test results as binary and independent, failing to leverage the sequential nature of testing to refine individual infection probability and guide isolation decisions. A formal probabilistic framework is needed to improve the interpretation of serial antigen and RT-PCR results, especially in the context of emerging antiviral resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Bayesian inference to update the posterior probability of infection after each sequential test result will yield more accurate, individualized infection status estimates than interpreting each test in isolation. This method will improve the positive predictive value of testing strategies, particularly when test sensitivity and specificity are imperfect or vary with viral mutations.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian updating model where the prior probability is based on the local prevalence (e.g., from Los Angeles County outbreak data) and individual exposure risk. Likelihoods will be defined using pre-2026 published test characteristics (sensitivity, specificity) for mpox antigen and RT-PCR assays, incorporating potential degradation from known resistance-associated mutations (e.g., tecovirimat resistance mutations). The model will be implemented using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling via the Stan probabilistic programming language to estimate posterior distributions after each test in a sequence. We will validate the model using retrospective testing data from the 2022-2024 Los Angeles County mpox outbreak.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, transparent tool for public health practitioners to interpret complex testing sequences, optimizing isolation protocols and resource allocation. It will directly address the challenge of case confirmation in outbreak settings, contributing to more precise surveillance. The framework will be generalizable to other pathogens of concern in LA County, such as hepatitis C or typhus, where sequential testing is employed.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods are known in diagnostics, their application to *sequential* outbreak testing in a real-world public health context, explicitly integrating local prevalence and mutation-impacted test performance, is novel. This moves beyond static test metrics to a dynamic, individualized risk assessment tool for frontline outbreak management.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a CRISPR-Cas12a-Based Point-of-Care Assay for Rapid Human Adenovirus Genotyping in Los Angeles County Outbreak Settings
proposals.motivation: Human adenovirus (HAdV) outbreaks in clinical settings, such as those documented in Los Angeles County for other pathogens, pose significant infection control challenges. Current HAdV typing relies on PCR and sequencing, which are slow and centralized, delaying targeted interventions. Rapid identification of specific HAdV types (e.g., types 3, 4, 7, 14) associated with severe disease is critical for implementing timely cohorting and infection control measures during outbreaks.
proposals.hypothesis: A CRISPR-Cas12a-based lateral flow assay targeting hypervariable regions of the hexon gene can accurately and rapidly (<30 minutes) distinguish clinically relevant HAdV types directly from respiratory swab samples at the point of care.
proposals.method: We will design specific CRISPR RNA (crRNA) guides for conserved flanking sequences and variable loops of the hexon gene from pre-2026 HAdV genome databases. The assay will employ isothermal recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) followed by Cas12a collateral cleavage of a reporter, visualized on a lateral flow strip. Validation will use a retrospective panel of characterized HAdV-positive specimens from recent Los Angeles County outbreak investigations, comparing results to standard sequencing. Performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity) will be calculated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, field-deployable tool for frontline clinics to perform rapid HAdV genotyping, enabling real-time infection control decisions during outbreaks. It will establish a framework for applying CRISPR diagnostics to adenovirus surveillance, directly addressing a gap in managing respiratory virus outbreaks in congregate settings.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR diagnostics have been explored for virus detection (e.g., SARS-CoV-2), their application for rapid, multiplex-free *genotyping* of diverse HAdV strains at the point of care is novel. This approach leverages specific crRNA design to exploit hexon gene variability for typing, moving beyond simple detection.

proposals.title: Wastewater-Based Surveillance for Adenovirus Outbreaks Associated with Eye Care Facilities in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, wastewater surveillance proved critical for early detection of community SARS-CoV-2 transmission. Concurrently, adenovirus outbreaks, particularly epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) linked to ophthalmology clinics, pose a significant public health burden, causing severe eye infections and clinic closures. Detecting these outbreaks traditionally relies on delayed clinical reporting, creating a need for proactive, non-invasive surveillance tools to protect patient safety and inform public health response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adenovirus DNA shed from infected individuals at eye care facilities can be detected and quantified in municipal wastewater, and that temporal and spatial analysis of this signal can identify facility-associated outbreaks before traditional case reporting.
proposals.method: We will adapt established SARS-CoV-2 wastewater monitoring protocols for adenovirus. Over 12 months, we will collect 24-hour composite wastewater samples from specific sewer manholes serving clusters of ophthalmology clinics in Los Angeles County. Samples will be concentrated, and nucleic acids extracted. We will use quantitative PCR (qPCR) targeting the adenovirus hexon gene for broad detection and a multiplex assay for type-specific identification (e.g., HAdV-D types associated with EKC). Viral load trends will be analyzed using statistical process control (SPC) charts (e.g., CUSUM) to identify significant increases. Spatial analysis will correlate signal spikes with known clinic locations and anonymized patient visit data.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish the technical feasibility of wastewater surveillance for detecting localized, facility-based viral outbreaks beyond respiratory pathogens. It will provide public health officials with a novel, early-warning tool for adenovirus outbreaks in healthcare settings, potentially reducing transmission. The methodological framework can be adapted for other pathogens shed in stool or urine that cause clinic-based outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While wastewater surveillance has been applied to community-level pathogens like SARS-CoV-2 and polio, its targeted application for detecting outbreaks originating from specific healthcare facilities, particularly for a non-enteric virus like adenovirus shed in tears, is a novel and non-obvious extension of the technology.

proposals.title: A Case-Control Study of Infection Prevention Factors Associated with the Absence of Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis Outbreaks in Los Angeles County Ophthalmology Clinics
proposals.motivation: Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), primarily caused by human adenoviruses, is a highly contagious and disruptive ocular infection frequently linked to outbreaks in clinical settings, leading to significant patient morbidity and clinic closures. Prior research by Terashita and others in Los Angeles County has focused on characterizing outbreaks (e.g., HCV, typhus, MPXV) but has not systematically investigated why some high-risk clinics successfully avoid outbreaks. Identifying protective, real-world infection control practices is critical for evidence-based public health guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: Ophthalmology clinics in Los Angeles County that have not experienced reported EKC outbreaks, despite high patient volume, implement a distinct set of infection prevention and control (IPC) practices compared to clinics that have experienced outbreaks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study. Cases will be clinics with a publicly reported EKC outbreak (2015-2025) identified via the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health (LAC DPH) records. Controls will be clinics matched by patient volume, payer mix, and geographic area without a reported outbreak. Using a structured survey instrument adapted from CDC and LAC DPH guidelines, trained interviewers will conduct blinded, on-site assessments of IPC practices, including hand hygiene compliance, surface disinfection protocols, instrument sterilization logs, staff screening policies, and patient triage procedures. Data will be analyzed using conditional logistic regression to identify practices significantly associated with outbreak absence.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond outbreak investigation to identify specific, actionable IPC factors that prevent EKC transmission in real-world clinical environments. The findings will directly inform tailored, evidence-based recommendations for ophthalmology and other outpatient specialties to mitigate future outbreaks. It will also establish a methodological framework for using public health records to identify 'control' facilities for comparative effectiveness research in infection prevention.
proposals.novelty: While outbreak investigations are common, this study flips the paradigm by systematically studying success—clinics without outbreaks—as a direct control group to isolate protective factors, a method not previously applied to EKC in this setting. It leverages the researcher's established collaboration with LAC DPH to access unique data for both case and control identification.

proposals.title: Regional Genomic Surveillance of Human Monkeypox Virus in Southern California Hospitals to Map Transmission Networks and Identify Emerging Tecovirimat Resistance
proposals.motivation: Following the 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak, sustained local transmission in Los Angeles County highlights the need for expanded genomic surveillance beyond single public health laboratories. Pre-2026 work by Terashita et al. identified tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in local Monkeypox virus (MPXV) cases, but the geographic scope and inter-hospital transmission dynamics in the region remain poorly characterized. Proactive surveillance is critical for detecting emerging antiviral resistance and guiding public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Expanding genomic surveillance to multiple hospital systems in Southern California will reveal previously unrecognized regional transmission clusters of MPXV and identify hospitals serving as hubs for the spread of variants with reduced susceptibility to tecovirimat.
proposals.method: We will apply the same whole-genome sequencing and phylogenetic methods used in the prior Los Angeles County study to MPXV-positive specimens from at least five major hospital networks across Los Angeles, Orange, and San Diego counties. Viral DNA will be extracted from residual diagnostic specimens, and libraries will be prepared for Illumina sequencing. Consensus genomes will be generated using the mpoxv1 pipeline, and phylogenetic trees will be constructed with Nextstrain and BEAST to infer transmission links. Mutations associated with tecovirimat resistance (e.g., in F13L) will be screened for using variant calling against the reference genome (NC_063383.1).
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first detailed map of MPXV transmission between major healthcare institutions in Southern California, directly informing regional outbreak response. It will establish a scalable framework for hospital-based genomic surveillance and provide crucial, real-time data on the prevalence and spread of antiviral resistance markers to guide treatment protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterized resistance in a county-level dataset, this proposal systematically links viral genomics to inter-hospital patient flow and healthcare networks, moving from case identification to understanding institutional pathways of spread. It operationalizes a distributed, hospital-led surveillance model beyond a centralized public health lab.

proposals.title: Rapid Metagenomic Sequencing for Outbreak Detection and Characterization of Emerging Pathogens in Los Angeles County Public Health Investigations
proposals.motivation: Public health responses in Los Angeles County, as evidenced by recent outbreaks of hepatitis C, fleaborne typhus, and mpox, are often delayed by conventional pathogen-specific diagnostics, which require prior suspicion of the causative agent. This diagnostic bottleneck hinders timely intervention and can obscure the full genomic context of an outbreak, including the presence of antimicrobial resistance mutations, such as those conferring tecovirimat resistance in mpox virus. Implementing a hypothesis-agnostic diagnostic approach is critical for improving outbreak detection and management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a rapid, on-site shotgun metagenomic sequencing pipeline for patient samples during public health investigations will significantly reduce the time to outbreak pathogen identification and simultaneously characterize key genomic features, such as virulence markers and drug-resistance mutations, compared to standard diagnostic pathways.
proposals.method: We will establish a workflow using the Oxford Nanopore Technologies MinION sequencer for rapid, portable sequencing of nucleic acids extracted from primary clinical samples (e.g., blood, lesion swabs) collected during active outbreak investigations by the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health. Raw sequencing reads will be analyzed in real-time using the EPI2ME platform with integrated pathogen databases (RefSeq, NCBI Virus) for taxonomic classification. For viral pathogens, consensus genomes will be assembled using minimap2 and Racon, followed by variant calling with iVar to identify mutations associated with antiviral resistance (e.g., F13L in mpox virus for tecovirimat).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, practical framework for deploying metagenomics in a frontline public health setting, directly contributing to faster outbreak source identification. It will generate critical data on the prevalence and transmission dynamics of resistance-associated mutations in circulating pathogens within the county, informing treatment guidelines and containment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While metagenomics is used in reference labs, its integration into the rapid-response workflow of a local health department for simultaneous outbreak detection and detailed genomic characterization of resistance represents a novel, applied public health strategy, moving beyond retrospective analysis to active outbreak management.

proposals.title: Implementation and Validation of a Real-Time Genomic Surveillance Pipeline for Early Outbreak Detection in Los Angeles County Hospital Admissions
proposals.motivation: Traditional outbreak detection relies on epidemiological data, causing delays in identifying and containing transmission clusters, as seen in recent HCV and fleaborne typhus outbreaks in Los Angeles County. The emergence of antiviral resistance, such as tecovirimat-resistant mpox, further underscores the need for faster, more precise surveillance. Real-time whole-genome sequencing (WGS) at the point of hospital admission could dramatically shorten this detection timeline, enabling immediate public health intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a rapid, on-site WGS and bioinformatics pipeline for targeted pathogens during hospital admission will identify genomic transmission clusters and resistance markers significantly faster than standard public health reporting, thereby enabling real-time outbreak detection.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective cohort study across three major Los Angeles County hospitals. For patients admitted with syndromes suggestive of notifiable infections (e.g., rash, fever of unknown origin), residual diagnostic specimens will undergo rapid, amplicon-based whole-genome sequencing using the Oxford Nanopore MinION platform for near real-time data generation. Raw reads will be processed through a customized bioinformatics pipeline (based on pre-2026 tools like Nextclade and UShER) for rapid variant calling, phylogenetic placement, and cluster identification. Genomic clusters will be cross-referenced with electronic health record data on patient location and timeline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, operational framework for real-time genomic surveillance in a clinical setting, directly applicable to future outbreaks. It will generate critical data on the feasibility and lead time gained by integrating WGS into admission workflows. The findings will inform public health guidelines for the early deployment of infection control and contact tracing measures.
proposals.novelty: While WGS is used for retrospective outbreak analysis, its application for *real-time* cluster detection *during* the admission process, specifically within the diverse and high-volume context of Los Angeles County hospitals, is novel. This moves genomic surveillance from a confirmatory tool to a frontline diagnostic and public health triage asset.

proposals.title: A Two-Tiered Diagnostic Strategy for Orthopoxvirus: Rapid PCR Screening with Targeted NGS Confirmation for Tecovirimat Resistance Surveillance in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2024 global mpox outbreak highlighted the need for scalable diagnostics that also monitor for antiviral resistance, as evidenced by the identification of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in Los Angeles County. Current reliance on NGS for all diagnostic steps is resource-intensive and slow for outbreak response. A more efficient, targeted approach is required for public health laboratories to rapidly identify cases while preserving the ability to detect emerging resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A diagnostic algorithm using a rapid, high-throughput orthopoxvirus PCR assay for initial screening, followed by targeted NGS only on PCR-positive samples, will accurately identify mpox cases and efficiently detect tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., in the F13L gene) without a loss of sensitivity compared to universal NGS.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a real-time PCR assay targeting a conserved region of the orthopoxvirus genome using primers/probes designed from pre-2026 sequences. Residual nucleic acids from PCR-positive clinical specimens previously collected during Los Angeles County mpox cases will undergo targeted NGS using a tiled amplicon panel (e.g., Illumina MiSeq) focusing on the viral DNA polymerase and F13L genes. Bioinformatic analysis will employ established pre-2026 pipelines (e.g., BWA for alignment, GATK for variant calling) to identify single nucleotide polymorphisms and confirm known resistance markers. The workflow's efficiency and accuracy will be compared to a reference standard of whole-genome sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, cost-effective diagnostic protocol for public health labs, enabling faster case reporting during outbreaks. It will establish a surveillance framework for tecovirimat resistance in real-time, directly informing clinical and public health guidance. The model can be adapted for other pathogens where resistance surveillance is critical, such as hepatitis C virus in outbreak settings.
proposals.novelty: While NGS is known to be powerful, this proposal specifically integrates it into a pragmatic, two-step public health workflow that prioritizes speed for case identification and reserves deep sequencing for targeted resistance genotyping, a tailored application not yet standardized for mpox. It leverages Dr. Terashita's established focus on Los Angeles County outbreaks and antiviral resistance to create an implementable model.

proposals.title: A Host-Based Immunoproteomic Signature for the Early Diagnosis of Mycobacterium chimaera Infection in Post-Cardiac Surgery Patients
proposals.motivation: Mycobacterium chimaera infections, linked to contaminated heater-cooler devices used in cardiac surgery, present a significant public health challenge due to their non-specific symptoms and long latency period, often leading to delayed diagnosis and poor outcomes. Current diagnosis relies on direct pathogen detection via culture or PCR from sterile sites, which is slow, insensitive, and often requires invasive procedures. There is a critical need for a faster, non-invasive diagnostic method to enable earlier intervention and improve patient prognosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with disseminated M. chimaera infection exhibit a distinct and measurable host immune response profile in peripheral blood, characterized by specific cytokine, chemokine, and antibody signatures, which can be detected prior to positive culture results.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using archived serum samples from patients in Los Angeles County who underwent cardiac surgery during the period of known heater-cooler device contamination. Cases will be patients with culture-confirmed M. chimaera infection; controls will be matched patients without infection. Using a multiplex immunoassay platform (e.g., Luminex), we will quantify a panel of 50+ analytes, including pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), T-cell associated markers (e.g., IFN-γ, IL-2), and chemokines. We will apply machine learning algorithms, specifically LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) regression for feature selection followed by Random Forest classification, to identify a minimal predictive signature differentiating cases from controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a novel host-response biomarker signature for M. chimaera, providing proof-of-concept for a faster, blood-based diagnostic tool. It will advance the field of infection diagnostics by demonstrating the utility of immunoproteomic profiling for slow-growing, difficult-to-culture pathogens. The findings could directly inform clinical protocols for monitoring at-risk post-surgical patients.
proposals.novelty: While host-response diagnostics are explored for acute infections like sepsis, their application to chronic, healthcare-associated infections like M. chimaera is novel. This approach leverages Dr. Terashita's expertise in outbreak investigation and resistance profiling to pivot from pathogen-centric to host-centric diagnostics for a persistent public health threat.

proposals.title: Evaluating Plasma Next-Generation Sequencing for Early Detection of Bloodborne Pathogens in Asymptomatic Patients Exposed to Contaminated Medical Devices: A Los Angeles County Case Study
proposals.motivation: Outbreaks linked to contaminated medical devices, such as the 2024 HCV outbreak at a Los Angeles pain clinic, often rely on symptom-based testing, potentially missing pre-symptomatic or asymptomatic infections. This can delay outbreak containment and treatment. The utility of broad, agnostic screening tools like plasma NGS in exposed but asymptomatic populations remains undefined but is critical for proactive public health response.
proposals.hypothesis: Plasma metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) will identify subclinical infections with bloodborne pathogens (e.g., HCV, HBV, HIV) in asymptomatic patients with documented exposure to contaminated medical devices more effectively than standard symptom-triggered serological testing, enabling earlier intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients identified as exposed during the 2024 Los Angeles HCV outbreak investigation. Using stored plasma samples collected during the exposure window, we will perform mNGS (using Illumina platforms and bioinformatics pipelines like SURPI) to detect pathogen nucleic acids. Results will be compared to the timing and results of subsequent clinical diagnostic testing (serology, PCR) obtained through the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance system. Statistical analysis will assess the sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value of plasma NGS versus standard of care.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the yield and potential clinical utility of plasma NGS as a screening tool in outbreak settings. It will inform public health guidelines on testing strategies for exposed populations. The findings could shift protocols from reactive, symptom-based testing to proactive molecular screening to prevent secondary transmission.
proposals.novelty: While plasma NGS is used for diagnosing febrile illness, its application for systematic screening of asymptomatic individuals in a documented point-source outbreak is novel. This directly addresses a gap in outbreak response protocols highlighted by recent local events.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Plasma Next-Generation Sequencing for Monitoring Treatment Response and Predicting Relapse in Mycobacterium chimaera Infections Following Cardiac Surgery
proposals.motivation: Mycobacterium chimaera infections, linked to contaminated heater-cooler devices in cardiac surgery, cause severe disseminated disease with high mortality. Treatment is prolonged (≥18 months) and complex, but current monitoring relies on insensitive culture and non-specific clinical signs, making it difficult to assess treatment efficacy or predict relapse early. A sensitive, blood-based biomarker for mycobacterial burden is urgently needed to guide clinical management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quantitative detection of M. chimaera cell-free DNA in plasma via targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS) will correlate with clinical treatment response and that persistent or re-emerging plasma DNA signal will precede clinical or microbiological relapse.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal case-control study using archived plasma samples from confirmed M. chimaera patients in Los Angeles County. Cases will be patients who relapsed; controls will be those with sustained cure. We will perform deep targeted NGS (using a custom panel for M. chimaera and the M. avium complex) on serial samples. Quantitative DNA load will be correlated with clinical data. Machine learning models (e.g., logistic regression, survival analysis) will be built to identify DNA kinetic patterns (e.g., time-to-clearance, rebound) associated with relapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the clinical utility of plasma NGS as a novel biomarker for M. chimaera infection. It will provide the first evidence-based kinetic thresholds for treatment response and establish a molecular method for early relapse detection, directly informing patient monitoring protocols. The analytical framework will be applicable to other slow-growing, difficult-to-culture mycobacterial pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While plasma NGS is used for cancer and some infections, its application for monitoring treatment of non-tuberculous mycobacteria, specifically M. chimaera, is novel. The integration of longitudinal NGS quantitation with clinical outcomes to define actionable monitoring thresholds represents a significant advance beyond simple pathogen detection.

proposals.title: Determining the Analytical Sensitivity and Specificity of a Plasma Next-Generation Sequencing Assay for the Detection of Mycobacterium chimaera
proposals.motivation: Mycobacterium chimaera infections, linked to contaminated heater-cooler devices in cardiac surgery, present a diagnostic challenge due to their indolent nature and the difficulty of culturing the pathogen. Plasma-based next-generation sequencing (NGS) offers a promising non-invasive tool for early detection and outbreak investigation, but its performance characteristics for M. chimaera are undefined. Establishing these metrics is critical for interpreting clinical results and implementing this assay in public health surveillance, as demonstrated by the researcher's prior work on outbreak investigations and resistance detection in Los Angeles County.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a plasma metagenomic NGS assay can detect M. chimaera DNA with a clinically relevant limit of detection (LoD) and high specificity, enabling its reliable use for diagnosing disseminated infection and informing public health responses.
proposals.method: We will use a well-characterized, quantified M. chimaera genomic DNA standard spiked into healthy human plasma at concentrations from 100 to 0.1 genome copies per microliter. Samples will undergo nucleic acid extraction, library preparation using a hybridization-capture panel targeting non-tuberculous mycobacteria, and sequencing on an Illumina platform. Bioinformatic analysis will employ the SURPI+ pipeline for alignment and Kraken2/Bracken for taxonomic classification. Specificity will be tested against a panel of plasma samples from patients with other mycobacterial infections and common respiratory pathogens. The LoD will be determined via probit analysis of replicate detection at each concentration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formally defined analytical performance metrics for a plasma NGS assay targeting M. chimaera, filling a key gap in the diagnostic toolkit. It will establish a benchmark for clinical validation studies and guide public health laboratories in implementing this assay for outbreak detection and case confirmation, directly extending methodologies from recent tecovirimat resistance surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While plasma NGS is used for pathogen detection, its rigorous analytical validation for the slow-growing, environmentally ubiquitous M. chimaera is novel. This proposal uniquely applies the precise, mutation-focused analytical framework from antiviral resistance studies (e.g., for tecovirimat and monkeypox virus) to the challenge of quantifying detection limits for a bacterial pathogen in a complex clinical matrix.

proposals.title: Linking Mycobacterium chimaera Dissemination to Tissue-Specific Origins via Cell-Free DNA Methylation Profiling in Los Angeles County Cases
proposals.motivation: Disseminated Mycobacterium chimaera infections, often associated with heater-cooler unit exposure during cardiac surgery, present a diagnostic challenge due to non-specific symptoms and difficulty culturing the pathogen from blood. Identifying the primary tissue reservoirs of infection is critical for understanding pathogenesis and guiding targeted therapy, but current methods are invasive. The rise of tecovirimat resistance in monkeypox underscores the need for advanced molecular surveillance tools for other pathogens like M. chimaera.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cell-free DNA (cfDNA) from patients with disseminated M. chimaera infection carries distinct, tissue-specific methylation signatures that can be computationally deconvoluted to identify the major tissue sources of bacterial release and replication.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome bisulfite sequencing on plasma cfDNA from a retrospective cohort of confirmed M. chimaera cases in Los Angeles County (2020-2025). Human cfDNA methylation patterns will be analyzed using a reference-based deconvolution algorithm (e.g., MethAtlas or a Random Forest classifier) trained on publicly available tissue-specific methylomes from the NIH Roadmap Epigenomics Project. The proportional contribution of tissues (e.g., lung, liver, cardiac tissue, bone marrow) to the cfDNA pool will be quantified and associated with clinical outcomes from public health records.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first non-invasive method to map the tissue tropism of M. chimaera in vivo, offering insights into its dissemination pathways. It will establish a novel cfDNA methylation application for bacterial infection surveillance, directly extending Dr. Terashita's work on outbreak investigation and antimicrobial resistance in Los Angeles County.
proposals.novelty: While cfDNA methylation for cancer detection is established, its application to pinpoint tissue injury in chronic bacterial infections is novel. This approach moves beyond pathogen detection to characterize the host's tissue-specific response to M. chimaera, a non-tuberculous mycobacterium of significant public health concern.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Plasma Metagenomic Next-Generation Sequencing Assay for the Universal Detection of Fastidious and Slow-Growing Pathogens in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostic workflows for fastidious or slow-growing pathogens (e.g., Bartonella spp., Mycobacteria, systemic fungi) rely on culture or pathogen-specific PCR, leading to significant diagnostic delays and missed cases, as evidenced by local outbreaks of fleaborne typhus and HCV. A universal, culture-independent diagnostic tool is urgently needed for timely public health intervention and patient management, particularly in diverse urban settings like Los Angeles County.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, optimized plasma metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) assay, coupled with a comprehensive, curated pathogen database, can reliably detect a broad spectrum of fastidious and slow-growing pathogens with sensitivity and specificity comparable to or exceeding standard-of-care diagnostics.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale a plasma NGS pipeline previously used for monkeypox virus resistance genotyping. The method involves extracting cell-free DNA from patient plasma, performing shotgun sequencing on the Illumina NextSeq 2000 platform, and analyzing reads using the SURPI+ computational pipeline for rapid taxonomic classification. A key technical innovation will be the creation and integration of a custom reference database encompassing genomic sequences of all known fastidious bacteria, mycobacteria, fungi, and parasites. Analytical validation will use spiked clinical samples, and clinical validation will be performed on a retrospective cohort of patients from Los Angeles County with suspected infections but negative initial cultures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scalable diagnostic assay capable of detecting hundreds of difficult-to-culture pathogens from a single blood draw. It will provide a blueprint for public health laboratories to implement mNGS for outbreak investigation and syndromic surveillance, directly addressing gaps highlighted by recent local outbreaks. The assay will also enable the detection of emerging antimicrobial resistance markers, as demonstrated with tecovirimat in MPXV.
proposals.novelty: While mNGS has been used for pathogen discovery in research settings, this proposal focuses on rigorous clinical validation and optimization for a defined, high-impact class of pathogens (fastidious/slow-growing) within a public health laboratory framework, creating a practical diagnostic tool rather than a research screen.

proposals.title: Host Genetic Susceptibility to Disseminated Mycobacterium chimaera Infection: A Case-Control Study of a Cardiac Surgery Cohort
proposals.motivation: Disseminated Mycobacterium chimaera infection following cardiac surgery, linked to contaminated heater-cooler units, presents a significant public health challenge with high mortality. While exposure is a necessary factor, the marked variability in clinical outcomes—from asymptomatic colonization to fatal disseminated disease—strongly suggests a role for host genetic susceptibility. Identifying these host factors is critical for risk stratification and understanding pathogenesis, mirroring the importance of host-pathogen interaction studies seen in other infectious disease outbreaks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific human genetic variants, particularly in immune-related pathways such as IFN-γ signaling and autophagy, are associated with an increased risk of developing disseminated M. chimaera disease following exposure during cardiac surgery.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study leveraging the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health's surveillance data and hospital records to identify confirmed cases of disseminated M. chimaera and matched exposed controls from the same surgical cohorts. Using whole-exome sequencing on host DNA samples, we will perform a genome-wide association study (GWAS) to identify single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with case status. Association analyses will be conducted using logistic regression models, adjusting for relevant clinical covariates, with significance assessed via standard GWAS p-value thresholds and pathway enrichment analysis using tools like FUMA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence for specific host genetic determinants of susceptibility to severe M. chimaera infection, moving beyond descriptive epidemiology. The findings could inform personalized surveillance strategies for exposed patients and elucidate key immune defense mechanisms against non-tuberculous mycobacteria, with potential relevance to other opportunistic infections.
proposals.novelty: This approach applies the 'inverse problem' framework—common in antimicrobial resistance studies (e.g., identifying viral mutations for tecovirimat resistance)—to the host side of a bacterial outbreak, shifting focus from pathogen genomics to host genetics to explain differential disease outcomes in a well-defined exposure setting.

proposals.title: A Case-Control Study of Protective Work Practices Against Fleaborne Typhus Among Pest Control and Sanitation Crews in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Fleaborne typhus, a vector-borne rickettsial disease, remains endemic in Los Angeles County, with outbreaks associated with environmental and occupational exposures. While prior research has focused on outbreak characterization and associated risk factors, there is a critical gap in identifying effective, real-world protective behaviors among high-risk outdoor crews. Understanding these successful practices is essential for developing targeted, evidence-based interventions to prevent infection in endemic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, modifiable work practices (e.g., personal protective equipment protocols, site-specific rodent mitigation strategies) and crew-level conditions are significantly associated with a lower incidence of fleaborne typhus infection among pest control and sanitation workers in endemic areas of Los Angeles County.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance data from 2020-2025. Cases will be workers with confirmed fleaborne typhus. Controls will be matched workers from the same companies without infection. Using structured surveys and site assessments, we will collect data on work practices, PPE use, and site conditions. We will employ multivariable conditional logistic regression to identify protective factors, controlling for potential confounders such as work duration and geographic location.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly identify actionable, protective work behaviors, shifting the focus from risk to prevention. It will provide public health officials and industry leaders with empirical evidence to refine occupational health guidelines for vector-borne disease prevention. The findings will create a framework for studying protective factors in other endemic occupational settings, such as for other zoonotic diseases.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the typical outbreak investigation paradigm by systematically studying successful prevention rather than just analyzing failures or outbreaks. It applies a case-control methodology, common in studying drug resistance (e.g., tecovirimat), to the novel domain of identifying positive behavioral and environmental determinants of health in an endemic occupational setting.

proposals.title: Industry-Specific Disparities in Measured vs. True Infection Risk: A Case Study of MPXV in Los Angeles County During the 2022-2023 Outbreak
proposals.motivation: Public health interventions and resource allocation rely on reported case incidence, which is heavily influenced by testing access and reporting completeness. The 2022-2023 global mpox (MPXV) outbreak highlighted potential disparities, where certain industries (e.g., entertainment, hospitality) may have had differential access to testing and care, skewing the perceived risk landscape. Understanding these biases is critical for accurate risk communication and equitable outbreak response.
proposals.hypothesis: Reported mpox case incidence across different industries in Los Angeles County is not proportional to the true infection risk, but is significantly confounded by industry-associated differences in healthcare-seeking behavior, testing access, and public health engagement.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective ecological analysis using Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance data on confirmed mpox cases from May 2022 to December 2023, linked with industry of employment. We will employ multivariable Poisson regression models, adjusting for demographic covariates, to estimate incidence rate ratios (IRRs) by industry. To assess bias, we will integrate anonymized data on negative test results and utilize capture-recapture methods to estimate under-ascertainment. Spatial analysis will map case clusters against the density of industries and testing sites.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the extent of reporting bias in a major metropolitan outbreak, providing a methodological framework to adjust observed incidence for industry-specific testing disparities. The findings will directly inform more targeted public health messaging and testing outreach strategies for future outbreaks, moving beyond crude case counts to a more nuanced understanding of transmission risk.
proposals.novelty: While occupational risk has been studied for diseases like hepatitis C, applying a bias-correction framework to the 2022 mpox outbreak to dissect industry-specific reporting artifacts is novel. It builds directly on Terashita's prior work on tecovirimat resistance and outbreak investigations in Los Angeles County by focusing on the social determinants of surveillance data itself.

proposals.title: Sector-Specific Transmission Dynamics of Influenza and RSV: A Retrospective Analysis of Workplace Outbreaks in Los Angeles County, 2015-2024
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the critical role of workplace settings in respiratory virus transmission, but systematic, sector-based analyses for influenza and RSV remain limited. Understanding how transmission dynamics vary across different workplace environments (e.g., manufacturing, offices, healthcare) is essential for designing targeted, non-pharmaceutical interventions and optimizing resource allocation for outbreak response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the incidence, attack rates, and temporal patterns of influenza and RSV outbreaks in Los Angeles County exhibit significant heterogeneity across economic sectors, driven by sector-specific factors such as indoor occupancy, ventilation, and workforce demographics.
proposals.method: We will apply the sector-based outbreak analysis framework previously used for HCV and MPXV to influenza and RSV. Using Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance data (2015-2024), we will geocode and link laboratory-confirmed case reports to workplace NAICS codes. We will employ negative binomial regression models to estimate sector-specific incidence rate ratios, adjusting for community prevalence, and use space-time scan statistics (SaTScan) to identify significant workplace-associated outbreak clusters. Transmission chains will be inferred using pairwise genetic distance analysis of available viral sequences.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, sector-specific epidemiology of influenza and RSV workplace outbreaks in a major metropolitan region. It will generate actionable evidence for public health officials to prioritize high-risk sectors for vaccination campaigns and workplace safety guidance. The findings will also validate a generalizable framework for respiratory pathogen outbreak analysis beyond pandemic coronaviruses.
proposals.novelty: While workplace studies exist for COVID-19, applying this granular sector-based framework, common in enteric and zoonotic outbreak investigations, to endemic respiratory viruses like influenza and RSV is novel. The integration of traditional epidemiology, spatial statistics, and genomic data for sectoral risk stratification represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Characterizing Environmental and Fomite Transmission Routes of Monkeypox Virus in a Los Angeles County Healthcare Setting: A Case-Control and Viral Sequencing Study
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak revealed significant healthcare-associated transmission, including cases without clear needlestick exposure, posing a major infection control challenge. Understanding the precise mechanisms of non-needlestick transmission—such as via contaminated surfaces (fomites), linens, or airborne particles during specific care activities—is critical for developing targeted interventions to protect patients and healthcare workers, especially in the context of potential tecovirimat resistance emerging in community settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific patient care activities (e.g., dressing changes, linen handling, or use of certain medical equipment) are associated with MPXV transmission via environmental contamination, and that viral sequencing will link healthcare-associated cases to distinct contamination hotspots within the facility.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study of confirmed MPXV cases among patients and healthcare workers in Los Angeles County acute-care hospitals from 2022-2025, matched by facility and time. Using detailed electronic health records and staff interviews, we will analyze associations between transmission and specific care activities, durations, and PPE use. Concurrently, we will perform prospective environmental sampling (swabbing high-touch surfaces, air filters) in care areas linked to cases, followed by whole-genome sequencing of recovered virus. Sequences will be compared to clinical isolates from linked cases and the Los Angeles County public health database to construct transmission networks and identify tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed epidemiological and genomic evidence linking specific healthcare activities to MPXV environmental transmission routes. It will generate actionable data for refining infection prevention protocols in hospitals and long-term care facilities. Furthermore, it will surveil for the introduction and spread of tecovirimat-resistant strains into healthcare environments, informing treatment and containment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified tecovirimat resistance mutations and described outbreaks in other settings, this study uniquely integrates detailed activity-based epidemiology with environmental genomics specifically within a healthcare context to pinpoint exact transmission mechanisms, moving beyond syndromic surveillance to mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: A Case-Control Study of Personal Protective Equipment and Occupational Monkeypox Virus Infection Among Healthcare Workers in Los Angeles County, 2022-2024
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox (MPXV) outbreak highlighted significant occupational risks for healthcare workers (HCWs), with transmission occurring in clinical settings. Concurrently, the identification of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in Los Angeles County underscores the critical need for effective non-pharmaceutical interventions. Evaluating the real-world effectiveness of specific PPE components is essential to inform evidence-based infection prevention and control (IPC) guidelines for MPXV and related orthopoxviruses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that consistent use of specific PPE components (gloves, gowns, eye protection, and N95 respirators) is independently associated with a reduced risk of occupational MPXV infection among HCWs in high-exposure settings.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance and case investigation data from 2022-2024. Confirmed occupational MPXV cases among HCWs will be matched with up to four controls (HCWs without infection) by facility, role, and time period. Using structured interview data, we will assess exposure history and PPE use for specific patient care activities. We will employ multivariable conditional logistic regression to calculate adjusted odds ratios (aORs) for infection associated with the use of each PPE component, controlling for confounders such as duration of contact and aerosol-generating procedures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide quantitative, real-world estimates of the protective efficacy of individual and combined PPE elements against occupational MPXV transmission. The findings will directly inform and refine IPC protocols for HCWs managing mpox cases, particularly in the context of emerging antiviral resistance. The methodology will establish a framework for rapidly assessing PPE effectiveness during future zoonotic orthopoxvirus outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While PPE use is broadly recommended, there is a lack of empirical, activity-specific data on its effectiveness against MPXV in clinical settings. This study leverages detailed public health case data from a major outbreak epicenter to move beyond generic recommendations and provide granular, actionable evidence on which PPE components are most critical for risk reduction.

proposals.title: Environmental Contamination and Viral Shedding Dynamics in Hospital Rooms of Patients with Mpox: A Prospective Cohort Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak highlighted significant knowledge gaps in nosocomial transmission risks, particularly for patients with severe or tecovirimat-resistant infections. Understanding the extent and persistence of environmental contamination is critical for informing infection prevention and control (IPC) protocols in healthcare settings, a concern amplified by Dr. Terashita's prior identification of tecovirimat resistance in L.A. County.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-touch surfaces and air samples in hospital rooms of mpox patients will contain detectable MPXV DNA, with viral load and persistence positively associated with the patient's lesion burden, location, and tecovirimat treatment status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of mpox patients hospitalized in L.A. County. Environmental sampling will be performed daily using pre-moistened swabs on high-touch surfaces (e.g., bed rails, tables, bathrooms) and air samplers. Viral DNA will be quantified via quantitative PCR (qPCR). We will use multivariable Poisson regression models to analyze associations between environmental viral load (outcome) and patient-level factors (lesion count, anatomic site, tecovirimat use, resistance mutations), controlling for time since admission and room cleaning cycles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, longitudinal data on MPXV environmental contamination in U.S. hospital rooms, directly informing IPC guidelines. It will also offer preliminary evidence on whether tecovirimat-resistant infections alter shedding patterns, a crucial question for clinical management.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies documented contamination, this work integrates detailed clinical data—including drug resistance genotyping—with longitudinal environmental sampling to model drivers of contamination, moving beyond mere detection to risk factor analysis.

proposals.title: Environmental Stability and Transmission Risk of Orthopoxviruses: A Molecular Study of Fomite and Aerosol Decay Using Surrogate and MPXV Models in Simulated Clinical Environments
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak, including cases in Los Angeles County, highlighted significant knowledge gaps regarding non-contact transmission routes, particularly fomite and aerosol stability in healthcare settings. Concurrently, the emergence of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in MPXV, as documented in LA County, necessitates understanding if such mutations alter environmental stability, potentially impacting transmission dynamics and infection control. This is critical for public health guidance in clinical environments, as seen in prior local investigations of HCV and typhus outbreaks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) MPXV exhibits significant stability on clinical fomites and in aerosolized particles, and (2) specific tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., in the F13L gene) alter viral surface properties, thereby increasing environmental persistence compared to wild-type virus.
proposals.method: We will employ a BSL-2/3 hybrid approach using vaccinia virus (a surrogate orthopoxvirus) and a panel of contemporary, sequence-confirmed MPXV isolates (including tecovirimat-resistant strains from LA County). Viral stability will be assessed on clinically relevant surfaces (stainless steel, plastic, cotton) and in aerosolized particles using a rotating drum aerosol chamber under controlled temperature and humidity mimicking hospital conditions. Viral decay kinetics will be quantified via plaque assay and RT-qPCR over 72 hours. Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects regression models to compare decay rates between virus types and conditions, controlling for environmental variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, comparative data on the environmental persistence of tecovirimat-resistant versus susceptible MPXV strains. It will directly inform evidence-based infection control protocols for clinical settings managing mpox cases. The findings will contribute to molecular transmission models by linking specific viral genotypes (resistance mutations) to phenotypic stability, a key parameter for outbreak forecasting.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have assessed MPXV environmental stability generically, none have systematically investigated the impact of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations on persistence. Furthermore, integrating surrogate virus data with contemporary clinical MPXV isolates in a controlled, simulated clinical environment bridges a critical gap between molecular virology and practical public health infection control.

proposals.title: Patient-Level Risk Factors for Nosocomial Transmission of Human Monkeypox Virus to Healthcare Workers: A Case-Control Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak highlighted significant occupational risk for healthcare workers (HCWs), straining infection control resources. Identifying which patients pose the highest transmission risk is crucial for optimizing protective measures and preventing nosocomial outbreaks, especially in the context of emerging tecovirimat resistance which may alter clinical presentation and transmissibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific patient-level clinical, virological, and epidemiological factors—including high viral load in specific lesion sites, delayed diagnosis, and infection with tecovirimat-resistant MPXV strains—are independently associated with an increased risk of HCW transmission.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study using Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance and hospital records from 2022-2025. Cases are MPXV-infected HCWs with a confirmed nosocomial exposure; controls are MPXV patients who did not transmit to HCWs despite similar exposure potential. We will use multivariable logistic regression to model the association between transmission and predictors including lesion location/severity (via chart review), time-to-diagnosis, tecovirimat use, and presence of resistance-associated mutations (from available sequencing data).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative assessment of patient-specific risk factors for MPXV transmission to HCWs, moving beyond generic infection control guidelines. The findings will directly inform risk-stratified patient management and personal protective equipment (PPE) protocols in clinical settings, potentially reducing occupational infections.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical HCW-focused risk analysis to model the 'source patient' characteristics driving transmission, integrating clinical data with molecular surveillance for antiviral resistance—a novel synthesis given the recent emergence of tecovirimat resistance.

proposals.title: Evaluating Tecovirimat Combination Regimens for Suppressing Resistance-Associated Mutations in Prolonged Mpox Virus Infections: A Retrospective Cohort Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak revealed severe, prolonged infections in immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with advanced HIV. Pre-2026 studies, including work in Los Angeles County, have identified specific mutations (e.g., in the F13L gene) associated with tecovirimat resistance, raising concerns about treatment failure. This problem is critical for public health as it mirrors historical challenges in antiviral monotherapy for HIV and hepatitis C, where resistance undermines control efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: Combining tecovirimat with a second antiviral agent with a non-overlapping mechanism of action (e.g., brincidofovir) will be associated with a lower incidence and delayed emergence of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in immunocompromised patients with prolonged mpox virus infection compared to tecovirimat monotherapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using integrated public health, laboratory, and clinical records from Los Angeles County Department of Public Health for immunocompromised mpox patients (2022-2025). Cases will be stratified by treatment: tecovirimat monotherapy vs. combination therapy (tecovirimat + brincidofovir). Using longitudinal viral sequencing data, we will apply survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) to model the time-to-detection of known resistance-associated mutations. We will control for confounders like HIV viral load, CD4 count, and treatment duration using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world clinical evidence on the efficacy of combination antiviral therapy for preventing resistance in mpox. It will generate actionable data for public health guidelines on managing complex mpox cases. The findings will also establish a methodological framework for rapid assessment of resistance emergence during future orthopoxvirus outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While combination therapy is a established concept in virology, its application to mpox and tecovirimat is novel and untested in clinical settings. This study uniquely leverages detailed pre-existing public health case data from a major outbreak epicenter (Los Angeles County) to answer this question with direct clinical relevance.

proposals.title: Development of a Fluorescent Reporter-Based Assay for Rapid Phenotypic Assessment of Tecovirimat Susceptibility in Clinical Monkeypox Virus Isolates from Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak highlighted the critical need for effective antiviral therapies like tecovirimat. However, as evidenced by recent reports from Los Angeles County and elsewhere, mutations associated with potential tecovirimat resistance are emerging. Current methods for phenotypic resistance confirmation, such as plaque reduction assays, are slow, labor-intensive, and low-throughput, creating a dangerous lag in clinical and public health decision-making during outbreaks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fluorescent reporter virus assay, adapted from established hepatitis C virus (HCV) research methodologies, can be successfully developed for monkeypox virus (MPXV) to provide rapid, quantitative, and high-throughput phenotypic assessment of tecovirimat susceptibility directly from clinical specimens.
proposals.method: We will engineer a recombinant MPXV expressing a fluorescent reporter protein (e.g., GFP) under a viral promoter, building on proven poxvirus reverse genetics systems. Clinical MPXV isolates from Los Angeles County, pre-screened for known resistance-associated mutations (e.g., F13L mutations), will be used to infect permissive cell lines. Virus replication in the presence of a tecovirimat dose-response will be quantified in real-time via fluorescence intensity using a plate reader, generating EC50 values. This will be directly validated against traditional plaque assay results for correlation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, rapid phenotypic assay to distinguish between mutations that confer true tecovirimat resistance and those that are merely genetic bystanders. It will provide public health laboratories, like those in LA County, with a crucial tool for near-real-time surveillance of antiviral resistance, informing patient management and outbreak containment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While reporter assays are standard for viruses like HCV, their application to orthopoxviruses for rapid drug susceptibility testing is not routine. This proposal innovatively bridges a methodological gap from the HCV field to address an urgent need in MPXV response, creating a functional pipeline from mutation identification to rapid phenotypic confirmation.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy and Resistance Risk of Pre-Exposure Tecovirimat Prophylaxis in Immunocompromised Populations: A Prospective Cohort Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak highlighted the severe morbidity in immunocompromised (IC) individuals, a group for whom post-exposure prophylaxis is often insufficient. Tecovirimat is the primary antiviral, but its prophylactic use in high-risk IC groups is not standardized. Concurrently, tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., in F13L) have been identified in treated patients in Los Angeles County, raising concerns that prolonged sub-therapeutic drug exposure during prophylaxis could select for resistant variants.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-exposure tecovirimat prophylaxis will reduce the incidence of mpox disease in a high-risk immunocompromised cohort but will be associated with the detection of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in breakthrough infections and asymptomatic viral shedding at a higher frequency than in a matched control group receiving standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study in Los Angeles County, recruiting 200 high-risk IC individuals (e.g., advanced HIV, transplant recipients) from infectious disease clinics. 100 participants opting for pre-exposure prophylaxis (200mg tecovirimat twice weekly) and 100 matched controls receiving standard care will be followed for 12 months. Serial nasopharyngeal and lesion (if present) swabs will be collected monthly and during any suspected infection. Viral whole-genome sequencing will be performed using Illumina MiSeq, with a bioinformatics pipeline (breseq, Bowtie2) to identify single nucleotide variants and indels, focusing on known resistance loci (F13L). Incidence rates and mutation frequencies will be compared using Cox proportional hazards and Fisher's exact tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world evidence on the effectiveness of tecovirimat prophylaxis in a vulnerable population, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will quantify the associated risk of resistance emergence, a critical piece of data for public health agencies like the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health to balance individual protection against population-level drug efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While resistance has been documented in treated cases, no study has prospectively assessed the de novo selection pressure of prophylaxis in a community setting. This integrates D. Terashita's expertise in outbreak investigation and resistance surveillance within a proactive clinical prevention framework.

proposals.title: Geospatial and Genomic Analysis of Local Exposure Risks Driving Human Monkeypox Virus (hMPXV) Case Increases in Los Angeles County Health Districts, 2023-2025
proposals.motivation: Following the 2022 global outbreak, human monkeypox virus (hMPXV) has established endemic transmission in Los Angeles County, with heterogeneous case distribution across its 19 health districts. Identifying specific, local exposure sources (e.g., specific venues, social networks, or animal reservoirs) is critical for targeted public health interventions, especially in the context of emerging tecovirimat resistance, which threatens treatment efficacy in persistent transmission settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that districts with the largest recent hMPXV case increases are driven by distinct, identifiable local exposure sources, and that viral genomic data will reveal transmission clusters and potential resistance mutations associated with these localized outbreaks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study integrating epidemiological, geospatial, and genomic data from Los Angeles County Department of Public Health (2023-2025). For the 19 districts, we will use Poisson regression to identify districts with significant case increases. For high-incidence districts, we will perform kernel density estimation (KDE) for case clustering and logistic regression associating cases with specific venues/behaviors from interview data. Concurrently, we will perform whole-genome sequencing of hMPXV from cases in these districts, constructing maximum-likelihood phylogenies to identify local transmission clusters and screening for tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., F13L, A288T).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide public health officials with a detailed map of high-risk exposure sources for targeted outreach and prevention. It will also contribute to understanding the genomic epidemiology of hMPXV in an established endemic setting, documenting the prevalence and spread of tecovirimat resistance mutations at a local level.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified tecovirimat resistance mutations, this study uniquely integrates fine-scale geospatial exposure analysis with viral genomics at the health district level to pinpoint the drivers of sustained endemic transmission, moving beyond national outbreak descriptions to local, actionable intelligence.

proposals.title: Syndromic Surveillance for Early Detection of Coccidioidomycosis Outbreaks in Los Angeles County: A Retrospective Analysis Using Influenza-Like Illness Data
proposals.motivation: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever) is an endemic fungal disease in the southwestern U.S., with incidence rising in California, including Los Angeles County. Current surveillance relies on laboratory confirmation, causing significant reporting delays that hinder timely public health interventions. Early detection of outbreaks is critical for targeting high-risk populations and initiating preventive measures.
proposals.hypothesis: Syndromic surveillance data for influenza-like illness (ILI), collected in near real-time, contains a detectable signal of early coccidioidomycosis cases before laboratory confirmation, allowing for earlier outbreak identification.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective time-series analysis using pre-2026 Los Angeles County Department of Public Health data. ILI syndrome counts from sentinel outpatient clinics and emergency departments will be obtained from existing influenza monitoring systems (e.g., ILINet). We will use aberration detection algorithms, specifically the Early Aberration Reporting System (EARS) C1-C3 and Farrington flexible models, to identify anomalous ILI increases in geographic regions and during seasons associated with higher coccidioidomycosis risk. These signals will be temporally cross-referenced with confirmed case data from the California Reportable Disease Information Exchange (CalREDIE) to assess lead time and correlation.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish proof-of-concept for repurposing existing syndromic surveillance infrastructure for a non-respiratory fungal disease, potentially reducing the detection lag for coccidioidomycosis outbreaks. It will provide the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health with a validated, low-cost method for early warning, enabling more proactive resource allocation and public communication.
proposals.novelty: While syndromic surveillance is well-established for influenza and bioterrorism, its application to detect endemic fungal disease outbreaks by leveraging non-specific respiratory symptom data is novel. This approach directly adapts methods used for acute viral outbreaks to a slower-onset, environmentally mediated disease.

proposals.title: Distinguishing Common Source from Convergent Evolution in Pathogen Outbreaks: A Genomic Epidemiology Framework Applied to Tecovirimat-Resistant Mpox in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Public health outbreak investigations rely on genomic data to infer a single, recent common source. However, the 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak and the identification of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., F13L, A288V) in Los Angeles County raise a critical question: could identical, resistant isolates arise independently from parallel environmental or treatment pressures, rather than a single transmission chain? Misinterpreting such convergence as a point-source outbreak could misdirect containment resources.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetically identical, resistant pathogen isolates collected during a defined outbreak can arise from independent evolutionary pathways (convergent evolution) under strong selective pressure, not solely from a recent common ancestor with subsequent clonal spread.
proposals.method: We will analyze publicly available human monkeypox virus (hMPXV) genomes from Los Angeles County and global databases (pre-2026). Using a maximum-likelihood phylogenetic framework (IQ-TREE), we will reconstruct time-scaled phylogenies. We will then employ ancestral state reconstruction (e.g., TreeTime) to infer the evolutionary history of known tecovirimat resistance mutations. Statistical tests for convergent evolution (e.g., CONSEL) will be applied to determine if identical resistant genotypes emerge on independent phylogenetic branches, controlling for geographic and temporal clustering using SaTScan.
