proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal analytical framework to differentiate between clonal outbreaks and convergent evolution in real-time outbreak genomics. It will directly inform public health guidance for managing antimicrobial resistance in outbreaks, particularly for pathogens like mpox where community transmission and treatment use are concurrent. The findings will refine cluster case definitions for outbreak investigators.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic clustering is standard for source attribution, proactively modeling and testing for the independent emergence of identical, clinically significant genotypes (like drug resistance) as an alternative hypothesis is not routine in public health outbreak pipelines. This integrates evolutionary models directly into frontline outbreak analytics.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Transmission Bottleneck of an Outbreak-Associated Pathogen: A Direct Source-to-Patient Metagenomic and Clinical Isolate Sequencing Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Outbreak investigations rely heavily on sequencing clinical isolates to infer transmission chains and identify resistance mutations, as seen in recent Los Angeles County studies of tecovirimat resistance in mpox and HCV outbreaks. However, clinical isolates may represent a severe sampling bias, capturing only a subset of the genetic diversity present at the infection source. This bias could obscure the full reservoir of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) or virulence factors in an outbreak environment, limiting our understanding of transmission dynamics and potential treatment failures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transmission bottleneck from a persistent environmental source to patients is severe, and that concurrent deep sequencing of the source environment will reveal a significantly broader diversity of variants, including clinically relevant mutations (e.g., tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations in orthopoxviruses) that are absent or underrepresented in sequenced clinical isolates.
proposals.method: We will identify an active, point-source outbreak in Los Angeles County (e.g., linked to a healthcare facility or contaminated water supply). We will perform longitudinal, deep-shotgun metagenomic sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) on environmental samples from the identified source. Concurrently, we will perform whole-genome sequencing of all available clinical isolates from epidemiologically linked cases. Variant calling on source metagenomes will use sensitive, low-frequency variant callers like LoFreq. Transmission bottleneck size will be quantified using a Bayesian framework (e.g., `bottleneck` R package) to compare the genetic diversity (SNVs, indels) in the source metagenome pool to the variants successfully transmitted to and detected in patients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative estimate of the transmission bottleneck from a characterized environmental source to patients in a real-world outbreak setting. It will determine if standard clinical isolate sequencing surveils the complete threat landscape, specifically for pre-existing AMR/virulence variants. The findings will inform public health guidelines on when environmental metagenomic surveillance is critical for outbreak management.
proposals.novelty: While source-tracking uses sequencing, directly comparing the full *in-situ* genetic diversity of a source (via metagenomics) to the diversity in clinical cases to quantify the transmission bottleneck is novel. This moves beyond phylogenetic linkage to a functional, population genetics question of what is *not* transmitted, which is critical for assessing hidden AMR risk.

proposals.title: Assessing the Representativeness of Clinical Isolates for Outbreak Strain Diversity: A Case Study of Tecovirimat-Resistant Mpox in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Accurate genomic surveillance of outbreaks is critical for guiding public health interventions, such as antiviral use. However, it is unclear if isolates from a subset of patients fully capture the genomic diversity of the outbreak strain, including minority variants that may harbor resistance mutations. This gap is particularly pressing for pathogens like human monkeypox virus (hMPXV), where emerging tecovirimat resistance, as identified in Los Angeles County, threatens treatment efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinical isolates from patients during an outbreak do not fully represent the genomic diversity of the circulating strain, potentially missing low-frequency resistance-associated mutations present in the transmission reservoir.
proposals.method: We will perform deep, high-coverage whole-genome sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) on a retrospective cohort of clinical hMPXV isolates from the 2022-2023 Los Angeles County outbreak. We will compare this diversity to that obtained from direct metagenomic sequencing of primary patient specimens (e.g., lesion swab nucleic acid extracts) from the same individuals. Diversity will be quantified using established bioinformatics pipelines (BWA-MEM, GATK) to call single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and assess allele frequencies. Population genetics metrics, including Shannon diversity indices and nucleotide diversity (π), will be calculated and compared between isolate and direct sequence datasets using paired statistical tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating bias in outbreak genomic surveillance. It will directly inform public health laboratories on the risk of missing critical minority variants, like tecovirimat resistance mutations, when relying solely on cultured isolates. The findings will strengthen the evidence base for best practices in genomic surveillance of emerging viral threats.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified resistance mutations in isolates, this study directly tests the assumption that isolates are representative, a foundational yet unverified premise of outbreak genomics. It applies a rigorous, paired-sample design from a real-world public health outbreak context to answer this methodological question.

proposals.title: Direct Whole-Genome Sequencing from Clinical Specimens for Surveillance of Tecovirimat Resistance in Mpox Virus
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak highlighted the critical need for rapid genomic surveillance to monitor for antiviral resistance, particularly to tecovirimat. Current workflows rely on viral culture to achieve sufficient viral load for sequencing, which is slow, introduces selection bias, and risks missing low-frequency resistance-associated mutations present in the original patient sample. This delay and potential bias hinder timely public health responses, as seen in Los Angeles County's efforts to track resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimized target enrichment and library preparation protocols, combined with advanced bioinformatic deconvolution, will enable accurate whole-genome sequencing of mpox virus directly from diverse clinical specimens, reliably detecting resistance-associated mutations without the need for culture.
proposals.method: We will develop a method using a custom, tiled probe panel (e.g., Twist Bioscience) for hybridization-based capture of mpox virus DNA directly from residual nucleic acid extracts of PCR-positive lesion swabs. Library preparation will be optimized for low-input, fragmented DNA using kits such as Illumina DNA Prep. Sequencing data will be analyzed with a pipeline incorporating human host read subtraction (Kraken2), reference-based assembly (BWA, iVar), and variant calling (LoFreq) to identify low-frequency variants. Performance will be benchmarked against the standard culture-based sequencing method.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, rapid protocol for public health labs to perform direct-from-specimen mpox virus sequencing, cutting turnaround time from weeks to days. It will provide a more accurate picture of resistance mutation prevalence and dynamics within patient populations, directly informing clinical and public health guidance on tecovirimat use.
proposals.novelty: While direct sequencing is used for other viruses, its application for comprehensive mpox virus surveillance, specifically tailored for resistance monitoring and validated against the complex background of lesion swab samples, represents a significant methodological advance over the culture-dependent standard.

proposals.title: Heterogeneity of Infection Risk in Healthcare-Associated Outbreaks: A Case-Control Analysis of Facility-Level Practices in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Public health surveillance often categorizes outbreaks by broad setting types (e.g., hospital, clinic), which may mask critical, modifiable risk factors. This is crucial for pathogens of high consequence, such as emerging viruses where antiviral resistance (e.g., tecovirimat-resistant MPXV) or difficult-to-treat infections (e.g., HCV) can spread in healthcare environments. Understanding facility-specific drivers is key for targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: Within a single broad setting category (e.g., outpatient clinics), the risk of infection during an outbreak varies significantly and is more strongly associated with specific, measurable facility practices and protocols than with the categorical label itself.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study of reported healthcare-associated outbreaks in Los Angeles County (2015-2024). Cases will be facilities with confirmed outbreaks (e.g., HCV, MPXV, typhus). Controls will be matched facilities without reported outbreaks from the same setting category and period. Using structured surveys and health department inspection records, we will quantify facility practices (e.g., injection safety, environmental cleaning, PPE use, staff training). We will employ multivariable conditional logistic regression to identify practices most strongly associated with outbreak occurrence, controlling for community incidence and patient demographics.
proposals.contribution: This work will move outbreak analysis beyond descriptive epidemiology to identify actionable, facility-level risk factors. It will provide public health officials with a evidence-based framework for targeted audits and interventions. The findings will directly inform preparedness for outbreaks involving pathogens with treatment challenges, such as antiviral-resistant poxviruses.
proposals.novelty: While outbreak reports describe settings, they rarely quantitatively compare risk factors *across* facilities within the same category. This study explicitly tests the assumption of uniform risk within a setting type, leveraging Dr. Terashita's expertise in detailed outbreak investigation and Los Angeles County data.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Healthcare-Associated Infection Attack Rates Among Healthcare Workers Across 27 Distinct Setting Types in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Healthcare workers (HCWs) face heterogeneous risks of infection depending on their work environment, but comparative, granular analyses across the full spectrum of healthcare settings are lacking. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Terashita's work on outbreaks in pain clinics and typhus, highlight that transmission dynamics are highly setting-specific. A systematic comparison of attack rates is crucial for developing targeted, resource-efficient infection prevention and control (IPC) policies beyond the hospital-centric models dominant before 2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HCW attack rates for key respiratory and contact-transmissible pathogens will vary significantly across 27 healthcare setting types, with outpatient procedural settings (e.g., pain clinics) and long-term care facilities demonstrating higher rates than general outpatient offices, independent of community incidence.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using existing Los Angeles County Department of Public Health surveillance and outbreak investigation data from 2020-2025. Cases among HCWs will be identified for pathogens including SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and MPXV. The denominator (number of HCWs at risk) will be estimated using a novel, setting-specific method combining California licensing board data, CMS provider files, and facility surveys. Attack rates will be calculated for each of the 27 settings (e.g., acute care hospital ED, skilled nursing facility, outpatient dialysis center, ambulatory surgery center). We will use multivariable Poisson regression, adjusting for county-wide temporal trends, vaccination coverage, and community incidence, to estimate adjusted incidence rate ratios.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first ranked, comparative profile of HCW infection risk across the continuum of healthcare delivery in a major metropolitan region. It will directly inform precision public health by identifying setting types requiring enhanced IPC support and resource allocation. The findings will provide an evidence base for revising occupational health guidelines and preparedness planning for future epidemics.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have focused on single settings or pathogens, this analysis integrates Dr. Terashita's expertise in cross-setting outbreak investigation to perform a simultaneous, multi-pathogen comparison across 27 distinct healthcare environments, a granularity not attempted in pre-2026 literature. The novel denominator estimation methodology moves beyond crude bed-count proxies to better approximate true workforce exposure.

proposals.title: Identification of Protective Practices Against Healthcare-Associated Transmission: A Case-Control Study of Los Angeles County Facilities During the 2022-2024 Mpox Outbreak
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2024 global mpox outbreak highlighted significant risk of healthcare-associated transmission, with documented cases among healthcare workers (HCWs). Concurrently, the emergence of tecovirimat-resistant mutations in Los Angeles County underscores the critical need for robust non-pharmaceutical infection prevention and control (IPC) strategies to protect HCWs and prevent the spread of resistant variants. Identifying facilities that successfully prevented HCW transmission can provide actionable evidence to strengthen IPC protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: Healthcare facilities in Los Angeles County that reported zero HCW mpox infections during the 2022-2024 outbreak implemented a distinct, identifiable set of administrative, environmental, and personal protective equipment (PPE) practices compared to facilities with documented HCW cases.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study. Cases are facilities with ≥1 laboratory-confirmed HCW mpox case (identified via LAC DPH records). Controls are facilities that managed ≥5 mpox patients but reported zero HCW infections. Using structured surveys and on-site audits, we will collect data on IPC practices, facility characteristics, and patient volumes. We will employ multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for confounders like patient caseload and facility type, to identify practices (e.g., specific PPE protocols, room ventilation, staff training) independently associated with zero HCW transmission.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a prioritized list of evidence-based IPC practices specifically associated with the prevention of occupational mpox transmission. The findings will directly inform public health guidance for future orthopoxvirus outbreaks, including those involving antiviral-resistant strains. It will also provide a methodological framework for rapidly identifying protective factors during ongoing outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While most IPC research focuses on failures and outbreaks, this study deliberately investigates successes (zero-transmission facilities) as a direct complement to Dr. Terashita's work on resistance mutations. It applies the case-control methodology, used in the researcher's prior work on typhus and HCV, to the novel context of identifying protective, rather than risk, factors for HCW infection.

proposals.title: Correlation of Symptom Resolution with Viable Virus Shedding in Tecovirimat-Treated Mpox Cases: A Longitudinal Cohort Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Public health guidance for mpox (formerly monkeypox) often links isolation periods to symptom resolution, assuming clinical recovery indicates non-infectiousness. This assumption is untested in the context of tecovirimat treatment, which may rapidly alleviate symptoms without fully clearing viable virus, especially given the emergence of tecovirimat resistance-associated mutations (e.g., F13L, A288V). Premature de-isolation could fuel community transmission, as seen in other outbreaks in Los Angeles County.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of tecovirimat-treated mpox patients continue to shed culturable virus from lesion swabs after self-reported symptom resolution, and that this prolonged shedding is associated with specific viral mutations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of confirmed mpox cases in Los Angeles County during a future outbreak. Participants will provide serial lesion swabs from diagnosis through 4 weeks post-symptom resolution. Samples will undergo quantitative PCR to measure viral DNA and parallel cell culture to assess for viable, replication-competent virus. Whole-genome sequencing will be performed on all baseline and a subset of follow-up samples to identify mutations, particularly in the F13L gene. We will use survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards) to model time from symptom resolution to culture negativity and logistic regression to identify factors (e.g., tecovirimat use, mutation status, lesion location) associated with prolonged viable shedding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, culture-based evidence to inform evidence-based isolation guidelines for mpox, particularly for treated cases. It will clarify the clinical and virological significance of emerging tecovirimat resistance mutations and contribute a methodological framework for assessing infectiousness in other viral diseases where symptom-based de-isolation is standard.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified resistance mutations, this study directly tests their functional consequence for transmission risk by linking genomics with longitudinal viral culture data in a real-world public health setting, challenging the symptom-based de-isolation paradigm.

proposals.title: Long-term Clinical and Immunological Outcomes in a Cohort of Early COVID-19 Patients: A 5-Year Follow-up Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, the long-term sequelae of COVID-19, often termed Post-Acute Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), remain a major public health burden with significant heterogeneity. The durability of immune responses and the clinical trajectory of patients infected during the initial waves of the pandemic are not fully characterized, limiting our ability to predict long-term outcomes and plan for healthcare needs. A longitudinal study of a well-defined early cohort is critical to understand the natural history of recovery and chronic illness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of early COVID-19 patients will exhibit persistent multi-system sequelae at 5-year follow-up, and that the magnitude and quality of the initial humoral and cellular immune response will be associated with the risk of developing long-term clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study leveraging existing public health infrastructure and data from Los Angeles County. The study population will be drawn from confirmed COVID-19 cases reported to the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health during the initial surge (March-July 2020). We will link these historical case records with subsequent electronic health records from county-affiliated hospitals and clinics to assess long-term clinical outcomes. Participants will be recruited for a follow-up visit involving a structured clinical assessment, pulmonary function tests, and collection of blood samples for advanced immunological profiling, including SARS-CoV-2-specific antibody titers (neutralization assays) and T-cell memory responses (ELISpot and intracellular cytokine staining). Statistical analyses will employ multivariable logistic and Cox proportional hazards regression models to identify factors associated with PASC.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first comprehensive, long-term (5-year) clinical and immunological follow-ups of a geographically and demographically defined early-pandemic cohort. It will quantify the burden of persistent sequelae and identify immunological correlates of protection against or risk for long COVID, informing patient management and vaccine strategy. The findings will directly contribute to public health planning for post-pandemic chronic disease burdens.
proposals.novelty: While many studies describe PASC, few combine long-term (>3 year) prospective clinical follow-up with deep immunological phenotyping in a cohort defined by infection during the initial, immunologically naïve phase of the pandemic. Leveraging the established public health case and surveillance infrastructure of Los Angeles County for longitudinal follow-up is a methodologically novel approach for this research question.

proposals.title: Assessing the Fecal-Oral Transmission Risk of SARS-CoV-2 in Los Angeles County: A Case-Control Study of Wastewater Viral Load and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has consistently detected SARS-CoV-2 RNA in stool and wastewater, with some studies isolating replication-competent virus. However, the epidemiological significance of this finding for a secondary fecal-oral transmission route remains unclear. Clarifying this potential route is critical for public health, as it could inform infection control guidelines in settings like long-term care facilities and for food handlers, beyond the established respiratory droplet precautions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals infected with SARS-CoV-2 variants exhibiting higher fecal viral loads and environmental persistence are associated with secondary cases where direct respiratory transmission is unlikely, indicating a potential fecal-oral transmission pathway.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study in Los Angeles County, leveraging public health surveillance data from 2020-2024. Cases will be defined as household or facility clusters where epidemiological investigation suggests non-respiratory transmission. Controls will be matched clusters with clear respiratory transmission. Using quantitative RT-PCR and viral culture on archived clinical stool samples, we will compare fecal viral loads and isolate viable virus. We will sequence the virus from stool and respiratory samples to identify mutations associated with gastrointestinal tropism or environmental stability, applying phylogenetic analysis to assess transmission links. Statistical analysis will use conditional logistic regression to model the association between high fecal viral load/viable virus and case status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct epidemiological evidence for or against the fecal-oral transmission of SARS-CoV-2 in a real-world setting. It will identify specific viral genetic markers potentially linked to gastrointestinal infection and environmental shedding. The findings will directly inform public health guidance for Los Angeles County and similar jurisdictions on managing risks in congregate settings.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have documented viral shedding in stool, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed clinical epidemiology, quantitative virology, and genomic sequencing in a case-control framework to directly test the fecal-oral transmission hypothesis, moving beyond correlation to assess causality.

proposals.title: Correlation of Serial Monkeypox Virus rRT-PCR Cycle Threshold Values with Clinical Severity and Treatment Outcomes in Los Angeles County, 2022-2025
proposals.motivation: During the 2022-2023 global mpox outbreak, the relationship between viral kinetics, measured by serial rRT-PCR Ct values, and clinical disease progression remained poorly characterized. Understanding this correlation is critical for prognostication, optimizing antiviral therapy duration, and interpreting Ct values in the context of emerging tecovirimat resistance, a concern identified in Los Angeles County.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that slower viral clearance, indicated by persistently low or slowly declining rRT-PCR Ct values from serial specimens, is positively associated with higher clinical severity scores and adverse outcomes, including treatment failure potentially linked to antiviral resistance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of laboratory-confirmed mpox cases in Los Angeles County (2022-2025). Serial Ct values from clinical specimens will be extracted from public health records. Clinical severity will be quantified using a composite score incorporating lesion count, anatomic site involvement, pain scores, and complications. Laboratory severity markers (e.g., LDH, CRP) will be collected. We will use linear mixed-effects models to analyze Ct value trajectories and correlate them with peak severity scores and outcomes, adjusting for covariates like HIV status and tecovirimat initiation. Cases with suspected resistance will be analyzed for distinct kinetic patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed analysis linking mpox viral kinetics to standardized clinical severity metrics in a U.S. outbreak setting. It will generate evidence to support the use of serial Ct values as a prognostic tool and inform clinical management guidelines, particularly for assessing treatment response in the era of potential antiviral resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified resistance-associated mutations, this study uniquely integrates detailed, longitudinal virologic data with comprehensive clinical scoring to define kinetic-clinical phenotypes, moving beyond case reports to a population-level analysis.

proposals.title: Modeling SARS-CoV-2 Transmission Dynamics in Los Angeles County: Adapting Influenza Serial Interval Estimation to Early COVID-19 Case Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate estimation of the serial interval (SI) is critical for understanding the transmission dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 and informing public health interventions like quarantine periods. Early in the pandemic, SI estimates were highly variable, complicating outbreak response. Leveraging established models from another respiratory virus, influenza, provides a robust methodological framework to derive more reliable, context-specific estimates from local outbreak data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established statistical models for estimating the serial interval of influenza, when applied to carefully curated early SARS-CoV-2 transmission pairs in Los Angeles County, will yield stable and epidemiologically plausible estimates that differ from early global approximations and better reflect local transmission patterns.
proposals.method: We will analyze line lists from early confirmed COVID-19 cases in Los Angeles County (e.g., January-May 2020) to identify infector-infectee pairs with clear epidemiological links. Using methods from influenza studies, we will fit a gamma distribution to the observed serial intervals via maximum likelihood estimation, accounting for interval censoring due to uncertain exposure times. We will compare results from this adapted model to early published estimates and assess the impact of different case ascertainment windows during the initial outbreak phase.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, locally grounded estimate of the SARS-CoV-2 serial interval for a major metropolitan area, directly informing local public health policy on isolation and contact tracing. It will demonstrate the utility of cross-applying established respiratory virus modeling frameworks to novel pathogens during emergent outbreaks, enhancing methodological rigor in early epidemiological assessments.
proposals.novelty: While serial intervals for COVID-19 have been estimated globally, this study novelly applies a mature influenza-specific modeling approach to localized, granular case data from Los Angeles County, integrating Dr. Terashita's expertise in analyzing county-level outbreak cases and antiviral resistance patterns into the foundational dynamics of disease spread.

proposals.title: Wastewater-Based Surveillance for SARS-CoV-2 Variants and Tecovirimat-Resistance Mutations in Los Angeles County: A Public Health Case Study
proposals.motivation: Traditional case-based surveillance for SARS-CoV-2 is resource-intensive and suffers from reporting lags and biases, especially as testing declines. Wastewater epidemiology, proven for polio and other pathogens, offers a passive, community-level tool for outbreak detection and variant tracking. This is critical for monitoring the emergence of antiviral resistance mutations, such as those seen with tecovirimat in MPXV, within a large, diverse metropolitan population like Los Angeles County.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, longitudinal sampling of wastewater across Los Angeles County can detect shifts in SARS-CoV-2 variant prevalence and identify the emergence of mutations associated with altered transmissibility or potential antiviral resistance earlier than clinical case surveillance.
proposals.method: We will establish a longitudinal sampling network at key wastewater treatment plants across Los Angeles County, mirroring public health jurisdictional boundaries. RNA will be extracted from 24-hour composite samples and SARS-CoV-2 will be quantified via RT-qPCR. For variant detection, we will employ a multiplexed, amplicon-based sequencing approach (e.g., ARTIC Network protocol) and analyze reads using the Freyja Bayesian deconvolution algorithm to estimate variant proportions. Sequences will be screened for known spike protein mutations and mutations in viral polymerase genes (nsp12) associated with altered fitness or potential resistance to therapies like remdesivir.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, scalable framework for integrating wastewater data into Los Angeles County's public health surveillance infrastructure. It will generate actionable, real-time data on community-level transmission and variant dynamics, directly informing resource allocation and outbreak response. Furthermore, it will pioneer methods for the early detection of antiviral resistance markers in wastewater, a novel application with implications for future respiratory virus threats.
proposals.novelty: While wastewater surveillance for SARS-CoV-2 exists, this proposal uniquely integrates it with the researcher's expertise in tecovirimat resistance and Los Angeles-specific outbreak investigations, applying a mutation-surveillance lens from MPXV to SARS-CoV-2. The focus on linking wastewater signals to specific public health jurisdictions and actions within a major metropolitan county represents a direct translation from environmental data to actionable public health intelligence.

proposals.title: Prevalence, Viral Dynamics, and Emergence of Antiviral Resistance in Asymptomatic Close Contacts of Confirmed Mpox Cases: A Prospective Cohort Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: The 2022-2024 global mpox outbreak highlighted significant knowledge gaps in the role of asymptomatic or paucisymptomatic infection in transmission and viral evolution. Prior work, including Terashita's identification of tecovirimat resistance in Los Angeles County, demonstrates the clinical reality of antiviral resistance. However, the reservoir for resistance-associated mutations and the contribution of asymptomatic infections to community spread remain poorly characterized, limiting effective containment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Asymptomatic close contacts of confirmed mpox cases harbor replicating virus with distinct mutational profiles, and a subset will carry minority variants associated with reduced susceptibility to tecovirimat prior to therapeutic exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study enrolling asymptomatic close contacts identified through routine public health case investigation in Los Angeles County. Participants will provide serial oropharyngeal, anal, and lesion (if any) swabs for qPCR testing and viral whole-genome sequencing during a 21-day follow-up period. Deep sequencing will be performed using a tiled amplicon approach (ARTIC Network protocol) to detect minority variants. We will use Poisson-Fitter and iVar algorithms to identify intra-host viral diversity and quantify mutations associated with tecovirimat resistance (e.g., F13L, A288T). Statistical analysis will assess associations between viral load, symptom onset, and mutation emergence using generalized estimating equations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal estimates of asymptomatic mpox infection prevalence and viral kinetics in a U.S. outbreak setting. It will directly inform public health guidance on testing and monitoring contacts. Critically, it will elucidate whether tecovirimat resistance can emerge and circulate in untreated individuals, a key finding for antiviral stewardship and pandemic preparedness.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies sequenced virus from symptomatic cases, this proposal systematically targets the under-sampled asymptomatic contact population to probe the origins of resistance. It integrates detailed public health case data from Los Angeles County with deep sequencing to link transmission chains with pre-therapeutic resistance evolution.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Transferability of Hospital-Based Infection Control Standards to Outpatient Clinics: A Case-Control Study in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Infection control standards are predominantly developed and validated in well-resourced hospital settings. The applicability and effectiveness of these standards in diverse outpatient settings, such as pain clinics or primary care offices, remain poorly understood, despite these being frequent sites of outbreaks (e.g., HCV outbreaks in Los Angeles pain clinics). This gap is critical as outpatient facilities often operate with different resource constraints, patient flow, and staff training models, potentially requiring tailored approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: Strict adherence to hospital-derived infection control standards is not associated with a reduced incidence of healthcare-associated infections in resource-limited outpatient settings, suggesting a need for context-specific, optimized protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study of outpatient clinics in Los Angeles County from 2020-2024. Cases will be clinics with reported outbreaks (e.g., HCV, bacterial infections) identified via public health records. Controls will be matched clinics without outbreaks. Using structured surveys and on-site audits, we will quantify adherence to a standardized hospital infection control checklist (e.g., CDC guidelines). We will employ multivariable logistic regression to analyze the association between adherence scores and outbreak status, adjusting for clinic type, patient volume, and staffing ratios. The analysis will identify which specific standards lack protective association in outpatient contexts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on the real-world effectiveness of hospital-centric infection control standards in outpatient environments. It will identify specific standards that may be redundant or insufficient in these settings, informing the development of more pragmatic, evidence-based guidelines for non-hospital healthcare. This directly addresses a gap in public health practice highlighted by recent local outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While infection control is well-studied in hospitals, systematically testing the transferability of these standards to diverse outpatient settings is a novel approach. The study leverages Dr. Terashita's established methodology of using detailed Los Angeles County public health data to investigate transmission dynamics in specific community settings, applying it to a foundational question of guideline validity.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of Post-Outbreak Infection Control Interventions in Los Angeles County Outpatient Settings: A Case-Study Approach
proposals.motivation: Outbreaks in outpatient settings, such as the 2022 Hepatitis C virus outbreak in a Los Angeles pain clinic, pose significant public health and economic burdens. Public health departments routinely implement interventions post-investigation, but their economic value is rarely quantified, leading to potential inefficiencies in resource allocation. A rigorous cost-effectiveness analysis is needed to inform evidence-based public health policy and justify intervention costs to stakeholders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cost-effectiveness of post-outbreak infection control interventions in outpatient settings varies significantly by pathogen (e.g., HCV, fleaborne typhus) and intervention type, and that a subset of commonly recommended measures will be highly cost-saving from a healthcare payer perspective.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cost-effectiveness analysis using a decision-analytic model (Markov model for HCV; decision tree for acute infections). The analysis will be grounded in three recent Los Angeles County case studies: the HCV pain clinic outbreak, fleaborne typhus-associated deaths, and tecovirimat resistance in mpox. We will build intervention scenarios (e.g., enhanced screening, staff retraining, environmental remediation, antimicrobial stewardship) using county public health reports. Cost data will be sourced from Medicare fee schedules, published literature, and county expenditure reports. Effectiveness will be measured in quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) and cases averted, with probabilistic sensitivity analysis to account for uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative economic evaluation of infection control responses across diverse outpatient outbreak contexts, directly informing Los Angeles County Department of Public Health budgeting and response planning. It will establish a adaptable methodological framework for local health departments to prioritize the most economically efficient interventions for future outbreaks.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist for hospital infection control, applying this methodology to the heterogeneous and resource-constrained setting of outpatient outbreak response is novel. Furthermore, integrating economic analysis with specific, locally investigated cases of bacterial, viral, and antimicrobial resistance threats provides actionable, context-specific evidence rarely found in the literature.

proposals.title: Genomic Epidemiology of Outpatient Outbreaks: Distinguishing Point-Source Events from Endemic Environmental Reservoirs in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Outbreaks in outpatient settings are increasingly recognized but challenging to investigate. Genetically identical pathogen isolates are often assumed to indicate a recent, single-point source, leading to focused but potentially misdirected interventions. This assumption may be flawed if persistent environmental reservoirs (e.g., contaminated water systems, equipment, or vectors) can seed genetically similar outbreaks independently across facilities, a scenario underexplored in diverse outpatient contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength of genomic evidence for a recent common source in an outpatient outbreak varies systematically by clinical specialty, with outbreaks in settings like dialysis clinics more likely linked to endemic environmental reservoirs than those in surgery centers, despite similar genetic relatedness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of three distinct outbreak types in Los Angeles County (2018-2024): HCV in surgery centers, bloodstream infections in dialysis clinics, and MPXV in general clinics. For each, we will integrate detailed epidemiological data (timing, location, patient linkages) with high-resolution whole-genome sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) of clinical isolates. We will apply phylodynamic models (BEAST2) to estimate time to most recent common ancestor and population structure. Concurrently, we will perform environmental sampling (surface swabs, water) at implicated facilities and sequence any recovered pathogens. Statistical comparison of genetic distances (SNP thresholds) within and between outbreaks, coupled with environmental detection, will test for independent acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, setting-specific risk model to guide outbreak investigations, indicating when to prioritize searching for a point-source breach versus investigating endemic contamination. It will provide concrete genomic and epidemiological thresholds for public health decision-making in outpatient settings, directly informing Los Angeles County Department of Public Health protocols.
proposals.novelty: While genomic epidemiology is standard, its application to differentiate between point-source and reservoir-driven outbreaks across diverse outpatient specialties is novel. The integrated comparison of procedural, dialysis, and general clinics using a unified genomic-environmental framework addresses a critical gap in understanding transmission ecology in non-hospital settings.

proposals.title: Serological Assessment of Undetected Mpox Virus Infections in Los Angeles County: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Quantify PCR Testing Gaps
proposals.motivation: PCR testing of respiratory or lesion swabs is the standard for diagnosing mpox (formerly monkeypox), but its sensitivity is contingent on sample timing, quality, and anatomical site. This can lead to missed infections, obscuring the true incidence and transmission dynamics, as seen in prior outbreaks like Hepatitis C in LA pain clinics. Understanding the gap between PCR-confirmed and total infections is critical for accurate burden estimation and for contextualizing the emergence of tecovirimat resistance, which may be underreported if resistant cases are missed by initial diagnostic efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of individuals infected with mpox virus during the 2022-2023 Los Angeles County outbreak developed a serological response but were never captured by PCR testing, and that these missed cases are associated with specific demographic or clinical presentations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective serosurvey using archived serum samples from two pre-defined cohorts in LA County: 1) high-risk individuals with mpox-compatible symptoms who tested PCR-negative during the outbreak, and 2) a random sample of individuals from sexual health clinics with no recorded PCR test. Using a validated, orthogonal two-assay algorithm (an mpox virus-specific ELISA followed by a plaque reduction neutralization test (PRNT) for confirmation), we will detect past infection. We will employ multivariable logistic regression models to identify factors (e.g., symptom profile, care-seeking delay, anatomical involvement) associated with being PCR-negative but seropositive, leveraging existing county public health data systems.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of PCR testing gaps for mpox in a major US metropolitan outbreak, directly informing the accuracy of historical incidence data. It will elucidate the clinical and epidemiological profile of missed cases, offering insights for improving future diagnostic protocols. Furthermore, it will establish a foundational seroprevalence baseline crucial for interpreting the true prevalence of emerging phenomena like antiviral resistance.
proposals.novelty: While serology has been used for mpox exposure studies, its targeted application to systematically quantify and characterize the cohort of infections missed by PCR during a documented outbreak in a US setting is novel. This approach directly bridges Dr. Terashita's expertise in LA County outbreak investigations and antiviral resistance with a critical methodological gap in infectious disease surveillance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Presymptomatic and Asymptomatic Transmission in the 2024-2025 Human Monkeypox Virus Outbreak in Los Angeles County
proposals.motivation: Understanding the dynamics of presymptomatic and asymptomatic transmission is critical for effective public health control of mpox, particularly given the potential for tecovirimat resistance to emerge. Prior to 2026, estimates of this transmission fraction were highly variable, complicating isolation and contact tracing guidance. Los Angeles County's robust public health surveillance and recent experience with mpox and tecovirimat resistance provides a unique opportunity to generate precise, actionable data.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion (>30%) of secondary human monkeypox virus (hMPXV) cases in the Los Angeles County outbreak were infected during the index case's presymptomatic period or by asymptomatic infected individuals.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using detailed case investigation data from the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health for all confirmed hMPXV cases from 2024-2025. For each transmission pair identified through contact tracing, we will calculate the serial interval (time between symptom onsets) and the incubation period. Using established statistical models (e.g., the Richardson-Lawson algorithm for interval censoring), we will infer the proportion of transmission occurring prior to symptom onset. Viral sequencing data will be integrated to confirm transmission links and assess for resistance-associated mutations in potential asymptomatic carriers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, locally relevant estimate of the presymptomatic/asymptomatic transmission fraction for hMPXV, directly informing the County's isolation and quarantine policies. It will also assess whether transmission from asymptomatic individuals is associated with specific viral lineages or tecovirimat resistance mutations, linking transmission dynamics to treatment pressures.
proposals.novelty: This analysis directly integrates genomic surveillance for antiviral resistance with detailed epidemiological contact tracing to quantify a key transmission parameter, a method not yet widely applied in mpox research pre-2026. It leverages a unique, high-resolution dataset from a major outbreak epicenter.

ideas.title: Assessing the necessity of a recent common source for outbreaks across diverse outpatient settings using genomic and environmental reservoir analysis
ideas.description: This research will investigate whether genetically identical pathogen isolates in outpatient outbreaks necessarily indicate a recent common source or could instead arise from independent acquisitions from persistent environmental reservoirs. It will systematically compare outbreaks in three distinct outpatient specialties—surgery centers, dialysis clinics, and general clinics—each with different procedural risks, patient vulnerabilities, and environmental exposures. By integrating detailed epidemiological data with high-resolution genomic sequencing and environmental sampling, the study will determine if the strength of evidence for a single common source varies by specialty. The outcome will be a setting-specific risk model that refines outbreak investigation protocols by indicating when to prioritize searching for a point-source breach versus investigating endemic environmental contamination.

ideas.title: Source-to-Patient Transmission Bottleneck Quantification via Concurrent Environmental Metagenomics and Clinical Isolate Sequencing
ideas.description: This study aims to rigorously test if clinical isolates represent outbreak strain diversity by directly comparing pathogen genomes from the infection source to those from patients. Researchers will perform deep metagenomic sequencing on longitudinal samples from the identified outbreak source (e.g., a contaminated food production facility or water supply) alongside whole-genome sequencing of clinical isolates from linked cases. By analyzing the full spectrum of genetic variants in the source environment and quantifying which subsets are present in patients, the study will measure the transmission bottleneck and identify any functionally important variants (e.g., antimicrobial resistance or virulence genes) that are present in the source but lost in clinical isolates.

ideas.title: Economic evaluation of genomic surveillance for detecting and containing cryptic fungal outbreaks
ideas.description: This research fuses economic evaluation with advanced genomic detection to assess the cost-effectiveness of implementing high-resolution fungal genomic surveillance in outpatient and inpatient settings. By adapting algorithms designed to detect cancer subclones, the study will identify minor, related fungal variants among patient and environmental isolates, revealing cryptic transmission chains missed by conventional typing. The core innovation is to model the economic impact of interventions (e.g., environmental decontamination, patient cohorting) triggered by this sensitive surveillance, comparing the costs of genomic programs to the costs averted by preventing outbreaks. This provides a data-driven framework for healthcare systems to justify investments in precision infection control based on both clinical and economic outcomes.

ideas.title: Transmission-Informative SNP Panels: Differentiating Donor Minor Variants from Recipient-Acquired SNPs
ideas.description: This research develops a minimal, cost-effective SNP panel specifically designed for initial outbreak screening, but with a crucial novel feature: the panel targets loci empirically known to be informative for transmission. It focuses on positions where SNPs are unlikely to pre-exist as stable minor variants in a donor population, based on an analysis of within-host diversity from deep sequencing data. This allows the rapid panel to filter out SNPs likely representing transmitted minor variants, highlighting only those that robustly signal a new transmission event or within-recipient evolution. The result is a scalable screening tool that provides more phylogenetically reliable data for initial outbreak investigation than standard targeted approaches.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory for Post-Outbreak Intervention Investment
ideas.description: This research adapts the core problem-solving framework from Financial Economics—specifically Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)—to the challenge of allocating limited resources after a healthcare-associated infection outbreak. The analogous problem in finance is allocating capital across assets with different risk-return profiles; here, the 'capital' is the post-outbreak response budget, and the 'assets' are candidate infection control interventions. The analogous MPT solution involves mathematically modeling expected benefit (akin to return, measured in infections or disability-adjusted life years averted) and uncertainty (akin to risk, from variability in effectiveness or implementation). By adapting this, the research will develop an 'efficient frontier' model for outbreak response, enabling decision-makers to visually and quantitatively compare combinations of interventions (e.g., contact precautions, UV disinfection, antibiotic stewardship) to identify the most cost-effective portfolio that maximizes public health benefit for a given level of investment risk, moving beyond simple cost-effectiveness ratios for single interventions.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Source Attribution for Outbreak Forensics: Testing the Single-Breach Hypothesis
ideas.description: This research develops a phylogenetic and modeling framework to test the hypothesis that fungal outbreaks originate from a single control breach. By applying and refining inverse models that predict donor genotype from recipient genomes, the project reconstructs the outbreak's source population diversity from observed cases. The core innovation is using the precision of this genomic inference to quantify whether the outbreak lineage is consistent with a single, temporally narrow introduction event or necessitates multiple, simultaneous minor breaches. This directly challenges a foundational assumption in outbreak investigation by providing a data-driven method to distinguish between single-point failures and systemic control vulnerabilities from recipient surveillance data alone.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Outbreak Detection Gap Through Multi-Stream Syndromic Anomaly Tracking
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a model to estimate the proportion of significant outpatient infection outbreaks that go unreported. It integrates real-time, anonymized data from digital health platforms, retail pharmacy sales, and ambulatory care networks to establish a syndromic baseline. The system identifies anomalies in syndrome incidence that lack corresponding official reports, providing a data-driven measure of under-detection. This approach moves beyond challenging the assumption to actively measuring the scope of the problem.

ideas.title: Unreported Outbreak Detection via Fungal Population Clonality Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the completeness of outbreak reporting by applying cancer subclonal detection algorithms to fungal pathogen genomics from routine clinical isolates. By searching for rare, shared genetic variants across isolates from different patients and locations, the method can identify cryptic transmission chains that have evaded traditional surveillance. This enables the reconstruction of unreported outbreaks, revealing their true scope and dynamics. Ultimately, it transforms sporadic clinical genomics data into a powerful population-level surveillance tool for fungal infections.

ideas.title: Adaptive Phylo-Surveillance SNP Panels (APSP) for Outbreak Screening
ideas.description: This system develops a dynamic, updating framework for targeted SNP genotyping in outbreak screening. It starts with a foundational core panel derived from population genomics but integrates a continuous learning pipeline that analyzes incoming global surveillance data (e.g., from GISAID, public health labs) to identify emerging variants and recombinants. The assay platform is designed to accommodate a rotating set of supplementary probes, allowing public health labs to periodically update their screening targets without changing the core assay chemistry. This creates a resilient, forward-looking screening tool that maintains low-cost, rapid deployment while mitigating the risk of obsolescence against evolving pathogens.

ideas.title: Generating Context-Specific Infection Control Bundles Through Participatory Design and Constraint-Based Modeling
ideas.description: This research abandons the premise of testing hospital standards as a benchmark. Instead, it begins by co-defining, with frontline workers and administrators in diverse outpatient settings, the operational constraints and locally acceptable trade-offs between infection risk and other priorities. Using these parameters, the study employs iterative simulation and design cycles to develop novel, minimalist infection control protocols tailored to each context. These candidate bundles are then implemented in a stepped-wedge trial, with evaluation focused on practical adoption, cost, and a composite outcome balancing infection rates with care accessibility and staff burden.

ideas.title: Minimal SNP Panels via Phylogenetic Anchor Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy, where minimal filter sets are designed from full spectral surveys to efficiently identify specific celestial objects. The analogous problem in microbial genomics is using costly whole-genome sequencing (WGST) for initial outbreak screening. The adapted solution is to develop a method to analyze WGST population data to identify a minimal set of 'phylogenetic anchor' SNPs—those positions that are most informative for distinguishing major lineages and detecting novel outbreaks. This targeted panel, designed for rapid, low-cost platforms like qPCR or microarrays, would serve as a triage tool, allowing public health teams to screen many samples quickly and reserve full WGS only for the most relevant or anomalous cases, effectively scaling down the resource intensity of initial surveillance.

ideas.title: Risk-Prioritized, Multi-Modal Surveillance for Pre-Outbreak Infection Control Failure
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple breach detection to implement a proactive, intelligent surveillance framework that predicts which infection control lapses are most likely to cause patient harm. It synthesizes data from electronic health records, inventory systems, staffing logs, and IoT sensors to generate a dynamic, facility-wide risk map. Using a machine learning model trained on historical near-miss and outbreak data, it assigns a actionable priority level to each detected anomaly, focusing human oversight on the most critical vulnerabilities. This approach transforms surveillance from a source of alert fatigue into a targeted, decision-support tool for preventing outbreaks before they start.

ideas.title: Outbreaks always originate from a single control breach

ideas.title: Convergent Evolution of Pathogen Genotypes: Distinguishing Common Source from Parallel Adaptation
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that genetically identical pathogen isolates in an outbreak necessarily indicate a recent common source. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where distinguishing homology from homoplasy is a core problem solved through phylogenetic comparative methods, we adapt this solution to microbial epidemiology. The adapted idea is to develop a diagnostic framework that analyzes 'identical' outbreak isolates not in isolation but within their broader genomic and phenotypic context. By searching for signatures of parallel adaptation (e.g., identical, highly specific adaptive mutations in otherwise distinct genetic backgrounds) and modeling the probability of independent acquisition from a persistent environmental niche, this research aims to provide methods to distinguish between a single point-source outbreak and multiple independent infections from a common, enduring environmental source.

ideas.title: Host-Neutralized Enrichment and Sequencing (HNES) for Direct Clinical Specimen Pathogen Genomics
ideas.description: This approach develops an integrated wet-lab and computational system to enable robust whole-genome sequencing of pathogens directly from complex clinical samples. It first applies selective host nucleic acid depletion combined with programmable, pan-microbial or targeted capture to enrich for pathogen genetic material prior to library construction. The method is optimized for minimal biomass input and maximized recovery of microbial sequences from samples like sputum, tissue biopsies, or blood. Subsequent bioinformatics pipelines are specifically tailored to assemble genomes from the resulting enriched, but still mixed, sequencing data, providing a complete workflow that overcomes the host background barrier.

ideas.title: Predictive Failure Detection in Outpatient Infection Control via Anomaly Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of predictive maintenance from industrial engineering to outpatient infection control. Just as factories use sensor data to predict mechanical failures, clinics can implement a proactive surveillance system using sensors to monitor key infection control parameters (e.g., hand hygiene compliance via dispenser logs, real-time adenosine triphosphate (ATP) bioluminescence readings on high-touch surfaces, and traffic flow through critical zones). A central analytics platform, trained on normal operational data, would use anomaly detection algorithms to flag emerging risk patterns—like a sustained drop in hand hygiene rates coinciding with increased patient turnover—that precede a breach. This enables infection preventionists to receive early warnings and intervene with staff feedback or environmental cleaning before an outbreak can initiate, shifting from reactive outbreak investigation to prospective risk mitigation.

ideas.title: Ultra-Deep Donor Variant Mapping to Categorically Distinguish *De Novo* from Pre-Existing Transmission SNPs
ideas.description: This study employs high-fidelity, ultra-deep sequencing (e.g., duplex sequencing) on the direct donor inoculum to create a comprehensive map of viral variants present at frequencies as low as ~0.1%. This donor variant map serves as a definitive reference to classify SNPs identified in recipient samples. By comparing post-transmission SNPs to this map, the research can categorically assign each SNP as either pre-existing in the donor (and therefore potentially selected during transmission or early replication) or genuinely *de novo* (arising uniquely in the recipient). This approach resolves the fundamental ambiguity of the original idea and allows for precise investigation of selection forces during transmission versus post-transmission adaptation.

ideas.title: Using Dark Matter Detection Methods to Estimate Unreported Outbreaks
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all significant outpatient infection outbreaks are detected and reported by drawing an analogy to dark matter detection in astrophysics. Just as cosmologists infer the presence and properties of invisible dark matter through its gravitational effects on visible structures, this research proposes to infer the presence and scale of unreported outbreaks through their indirect effects on observable, non-specific health and behavioral data. By adapting these indirect detection methods, we can develop statistical and modeling frameworks to estimate the true scope of the problem, mapping the 'dark matter' of undetected disease transmission hidden beneath the visible landscape of reported outbreaks.

ideas.title: Genomic Exclusion Thresholds via Signal Detection Theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the 'noise exclusion' problem from signal processing (specifically, radar detection) to the genomic epidemiology context. In radar, Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detectors dynamically set signal thresholds based on local background noise to exclude clutter and confidently identify only true targets. The analogous solution is to not use a single, fixed genomic distance (e.g., 5 SNPs) to rule out outbreak linkage. Instead, the research would develop a method to define dynamic 'exclusion thresholds' based on the background genomic diversity of the specific bacterial species and the relevant epidemiological context (e.g., hospital, community, geographic region). By modeling this baseline variation, a statistical threshold can be established; isolates whose genomic distance from a suspected outbreak cluster significantly exceeds this population-derived threshold can be confidently excluded from the outbreak, improving the specificity of outbreak investigations and scaling analysis by efficiently ruling out unrelated cases.

ideas.title: A hybrid compartmental-phylodynamic model integrating environmental and host reservoirs for C. immitis
ideas.description: This research develops a novel phylodynamic framework tailored for environmentally transmitted pathogens by integrating a structured epidemiological model with genomic sequence data. The model explicitly represents the soil reservoir and host populations as separate but connected compartments, allowing estimation of environmental persistence times and host exposure rates from genetic data. This approach overcomes the fatal flaw of direct viral model transfer by accommodating the unique life cycle and transmission ecology of Coccidioides. The resulting system provides robust estimates of transmission timing and rates that are ecologically plausible and can be validated against independent outbreak data.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Reconstruction of a C. immitis Transmission Bottleneck Using Multi-Recipient Sequencing
ideas.description: This study adapts solutions from viral phylodynamics to track genomic evolution in C. immitis during a point-source, donor-to-multiple-recipient outbreak. The analogous approach, used in HIV epidemiology, involves deep sequencing the pathogen population from a single source and all recipients to reconstruct transmission networks and quantify genetic bottlenecks. The adapted idea is to perform deep whole-genome sequencing of C. immitis isolates from the single donor (capturing within-host diversity) and from multiple infected recipients. By comparing these populations phylogenetically, we can reconstruct the transmission tree, estimate the effective founding population size for each recipient (the bottleneck), and distinguish between donor-derived mutations and those that arise independently during early adaptation in each new host. This will reveal the genomic consequences of a transmission bottleneck in a fungal pathogen.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Genomics for Pathogens: Direct WGST from Clinical Samples via Microfluidics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from human cancer genomics, where single-cell sequencing technologies bypass the need for tissue culture to profile tumor heterogeneity directly from biopsies. The analogous solution uses droplet-based microfluidics to isolate and barcode individual cells. Adapted to the clinical microbiology context, the method would encapsulate individual microbial cells from patient specimens in microfluidic droplets, lyse them, and perform targeted whole-genome amplification and sequencing. This approach would enable direct, culture-free Whole-Genome Sequencing and Typing (WGST) of pathogens, capturing genetic diversity and identifying mixed infections or minority resistant clones that are lost during standard culture isolation.

ideas.title: Modeling the Synergistic Threshold: System States and Breach Sequences that Catalyze Outbreaks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond counting breaches to model the complex preconditions for outbreak emergence. It uses computational simulation to treat the containment system as a dynamic network, where minor breaches are modeled as events that alter system states. The core investigation identifies the specific sequences, combinations, and timing of breaches that interact with latent environmental factors (e.g., host susceptibility gradients, pathogen load accumulation) to cross a critical failure threshold. The outcome is a framework for predicting not just if, but when and how, a series of minor incidents will synergistically escalate into a full-scale outbreak.

ideas.title: Probabilistic genomic exclusion framework integrating evolutionary and epidemiological context
ideas.description: This system moves beyond fixed SNP thresholds to a Bayesian model that estimates the probability that two isolates are unlinked given their observed genomic distance. It incorporates prior distributions for mutation rates and population diversity, which can be adjusted for specific pathogen traits (e.g., hypermutator status) and the plausible transmission window. By outputting a probability of exclusion, it provides a statistically rigorous and context-aware method to rule out isolates from an outbreak, preventing false exclusions due to atypical evolutionary dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Develop Context-Specific Infection Control Protocols
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that hospital-derived infection control standards are universally optimal. By drawing an analogy from Ecology and Conservation Biology—where the failure of applying a single management strategy from a resource-rich protected area to all ecosystems led to the adoption of context-specific strategies based on the 'ecological niche' concept—this idea proposes a novel approach. The adapted research would treat diverse outpatient settings as distinct niches. It would deconstruct hospital standards into core functional principles and develop a translation methodology to create context-specific protocols that achieve equivalent risk reduction within local resource and operational constraints, thereby testing if optimality is achieved through adaptation rather than direct application.

ideas.title: Comparative Outbreak Ecology: Applying Ecosystem Analysis to Outpatient Specialty Transmission Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the comparative ecosystem analysis framework from ecology to systematically analyze outbreak characteristics across outpatient specialties. The analogous problem in ecology involves understanding why similar disturbances (e.g., wildfires, invasive species) produce different patterns in forests versus grasslands versus wetlands, solved by analyzing system-specific structures and functions. Here, outpatient surgery centers, dialysis units, and general clinics are conceptualized as distinct healthcare ecosystems. The adapted study will map and compare each ecosystem's key properties—such as host susceptibility density (patient turnover), network connectivity (staff and patient movement), 'resource' flows (medications, blood, instruments), and procedural 'stressors' (invasive vs. non-invasive care). By applying this framework, the research aims to identify which structural or functional elements of each specialty's ecosystem most strongly predict specific outbreak sources (e.g., waterborne in dialysis, airborne in clinics, instrument-related in surgery) and transmission patterns, leading to more targeted, ecology-informed prevention strategies.

ideas.title: Outbreaks as a Consequence of Systemic Brittleness: A Resilience Engineering Approach
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that outbreaks originate from a single, major control breach. By drawing an analogy from Resilience Engineering and Systems Safety—specifically the Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation—the idea is adapted to propose that outbreaks are more likely the result of multiple, simultaneous minor breaches or latent weaknesses across a system's defensive layers. The analogous solution shifts focus from finding a single point of failure to analyzing the system's 'brittleness.' The adapted research idea involves developing a framework to model outbreak-prone systems (e.g., healthcare facilities, agricultural supply chains) as multi-layered defenses, then using forensic and prospective methods to identify how combinations of small, often normalized, failures align to create a pathway for pathogen transmission. This moves outbreak investigation from a linear 'cause-finding' mission to a systemic analysis of resilience and vulnerability.

ideas.title: Adapting Viral Subtyping Frameworks for Fungal Genotyping
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from virology to the problem of standardizing C. immitis genotyping. In virology, standardized subtyping systems for variable viruses (e.g., influenza) are built on conserved genomic segments that provide a stable backbone for classification. We adapt this by identifying a panel of conserved core genes within the C. immitis genome that are strategically located near variable regions involved in host interaction or adaptation. The solution involves implementing a core genome MLST scheme based on these stable loci to define primary strain types, while systematically documenting the associated alleles in the adjacent variable zones. This creates a unified, hierarchical genotyping framework that combines the reproducibility of core genome MLST with informative data on key polymorphic sites, facilitating clearer phylogenetic analysis and outbreak tracking.

ideas.title: Integrated Genomic-Epidemiological Modeling to Decipher Contemporary C. immitis Dispersal
ideas.description: This redesigned system performs pan-genomic analysis on a global isolate collection but crucially couples it with anonymized human mobility datasets and patient travel histories where available. It uses this integrated data to build and test spatial-genetic models that can distinguish deep historical population structure from recent, rapid dispersal events linked to modern travel and trade networks. The approach will identify genomic signatures of recent expansion and statistically link them to specific hypothesized corridors of spread, transforming a descriptive population study into a tool for forecasting and mitigating future outbreaks.

ideas.title: Adapting Microbial Strain Typing Algorithms for Fungal Subclonal Detection
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from microbial ecology and metagenomics. In that field, algorithms like StrainPhlAn and DESMAN solve the analogous problem of detecting minor, closely related bacterial strains within complex community sequencing data. They do this by identifying co-occurring single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and patterns of genetic linkage. This solution can be adapted to the context of donor-derived fungal isolates by applying similar computational frameworks to fungal genomic data. The adapted tools would deconvolve mixed sequencing reads to detect linked variant sets, thereby identifying and quantifying distinct fungal subclonal populations that may be present at low frequencies, mirroring the detection of cancer subclones but using methods pioneered for bacterial strain typing.

ideas.title: Apply coalescent skyline models from population genetics to estimate C. immitis transmission dynamics
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from population genetics, where coalescent skyline models are a standard tool for inferring historical demographic changes from genetic data. The analogous solution involves using the distribution of coalescent events in a genealogy (built from contemporary sequences) within a Bayesian framework to estimate a time-varying effective population size (Ne). We adapt this solution to the fungal pathogen C. immitis by treating its transmission history as a demographic process. The adapted research idea is to apply the Bayesian Skyline Plot framework to C. immitis genomic data, interpreting the estimated effective population size trajectory as a proxy for changes in the effective number of infections through time. This would allow estimation of transmission timing and relative rates from genomic data alone, identifying past epidemic expansions or bottlenecks in the pathogen's spread across endemic regions.

ideas.title: A Phased Handoff Framework: Measuring Outbreak Investigation Success by Actionable Resilience Commitments and Their Implementation
ideas.description: This system redefines outbreak investigation success not by long-term outcomes, but by its capacity to generate and formally transfer specific, actionable interventions that build resilience. At investigation close, the team produces a mandatory 'Resilience Handoff' document containing verifiable commitments (e.g., 'regulatory agency X will implement protocol Y by date Z'). Success is measured in phases: Phase 1 assesses source identification and handoff quality; Phase 2 audits the implementation of commitments by responsible bodies; Phase 3 monitors system performance under stress. This creates accountable, measurable short-term proxies for long-term resilience, ensuring the investigation's work is concretely linked to systemic change without being held hostage to unforeseeable future events.

ideas.title: Pan-genome-informed, optimized cgMLST scheme for high-resolution C. immitis genotyping
ideas.description: This approach begins by constructing a comprehensive pan-genome from a large, diverse collection of C. immitis genomes to map total genetic diversity. A pan-GWAS is then performed to identify genomic loci with variation strongly associated with epidemiological metadata like geographic origin or outbreak cluster. The final, optimized cgMLST scheme is defined from a curated set of these high-value loci, ensuring it captures the resolution needed for actionable molecular epidemiology, overcoming the limitation of a strictly conserved core genome.

ideas.title: From Root Cause to System Health: Adapting Reliability Engineering's RCM to Outbreak Response
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that outbreak investigation success is measured primarily by identifying a source. It draws an analogy from Reliability Engineering, specifically Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM), which solved the analogous problem of moving beyond simply fixing broken parts to preventing system failures. RCM systematically analyzes system functions, how they fail, and prioritizes maintenance to ensure function and resilience. Adapted to outbreak response, this means redefining success to include preventing recurrence and improving systemic resilience. The research would develop a framework to define public health system functions, analyze functional failures during an outbreak, and generate a prioritized set of post-outbreak interventions aimed at strengthening the system's core capabilities, moving the success metric from a discrete endpoint (source found) to a continuous improvement in system health.

ideas.title: Adapting Audio Source Separation to Deconvolute Fungal Strain Mixtures
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from digital signal processing, specifically audio source separation, to solve the problem of deconvoluting mixed C. immitis infections from a single clinical sample. In audio engineering, techniques like Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and non-negative matrix factorization are used to isolate individual sound sources (e.g., speakers, instruments) from a single mixed recording by identifying statistically independent components or characteristic spectral patterns. The adapted research idea applies this conceptual framework to genomic data: by treating sequencing reads from a polyfungal infection as a mixed signal, computational methods can be used to separate the constituent fungal strains. This would involve identifying and quantifying the unique genetic variant profiles of each strain as independent 'source signals' within the combined data, enabling strain-level resolution from a single, complex clinical sample without the need for prior isolation or culturing.
