ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Soil Provenance Methods for C. immitis Reservoir Tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from forensic geoscience, specifically soil provenance techniques, to the problem of tracing C. immitis in environmental reservoirs. In forensics, soil samples are sourced to specific locations using a multi-marker geochemical fingerprint (stable isotopes, trace elements, mineralogy). The adapted research idea applies this framework by developing a composite geogenomic signature for C. immitis. This signature would integrate stable isotope ratios (e.g., from fungal chitin), trace metal profiles absorbed from the local soil, and population-specific genomic SNP markers. The resulting multi-parameter fingerprint could then be used in microbial source tracking to link clinical cases or environmental detections to precise geographic reservoirs, such as specific rodent burrow networks or defined soil ecotypes, greatly enhancing our understanding of pathogen ecology and outbreak sources.

ideas.title: Genomic Surveillance as Seismic Network: Detecting Cryptic Outbreaks Through Anomaly Detection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that epidemiologic links must precede genomic confirmation by adapting a solution from geophysics. In seismology, networks detect earthquakes by analyzing continuous waveform data for anomalous clusters of small events, identifying impending major quakes without prior geological suspicion. Similarly, we propose treating routine, population-level Whole Genome Sequencing (WGST) data as a continuous surveillance 'waveform.' By developing algorithms to filter expected background genetic variation and detect subtle, spatially clustered anomalies in pathogen genomes—analogous to seismic tremors—we can identify cryptic outbreaks based solely on genomic signals. This would enable public health agencies to investigate genomic 'tremors' as early warnings of emerging outbreaks, potentially intervening before traditional epidemiological links are even suspected.

ideas.title: Modeling Breach-Type Evolution: From Latent Systemic Risk to Widespread Contagion
ideas.description: This research investigates the conditions under which initially rare or novel breach types can trigger severe, widespread outbreaks by evolving into dominant attack vectors. It employs agent-based and network contagion models to simulate how adversaries adapt and propagate successful novel breaches across digital ecosystems, factoring in system interdependencies and defensive responses. The outcome is a dynamic risk assessment framework that identifies high-potential 'breach evolution pathways' and the critical systemic vulnerabilities that enable them, moving beyond static frequency-severity comparisons to proactive threat forecasting.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Monitoring to Track Post-Outbreak Control Measure Evolution
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the long-term plot monitoring methods from ecology to the problem of tracking post-outbreak control measures. In ecology, permanent plots are used to systematically track ecosystem succession and stability after a major disturbance. Adapted to public health, the solution is to establish a formalized, long-term observational registry for key sites where control measures were implemented. Researchers would conduct standardized, periodic assessments at defined intervals (e.g., annually for 5 years, then biennially) to collect data on measure fidelity, contextual adaptations, resource sustainability, and effectiveness indicators. This structured, longitudinal approach moves beyond one-off evaluations to model the 'lifecycle' and eventual successional state (e.g., abandoned, maintained, institutionalized) of outbreak control interventions.

ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem as a probabilistic inference task within a constrained, multi-source framework. It requires collecting pre-transplant samples from both donor and recipient sites, as well as environmental controls, to establish prior genotype distributions. Longitudinal metagenomic sequencing of the recipient post-transplant is then used to model strain dynamics. A unified Bayesian model integrates all these data sources to jointly infer the donor's original fungal genotype, rigorously quantifying uncertainty and overcoming the identifiability issues of the original formulation.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Risk Models to Cybersecurity: Quantifying the Impact-Rarity of Breach Types
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that the most frequent type of security breach represents the most critical risk. By drawing an analogy from seismology, where the greatest structural and societal risks come from rare, high-magnitude earthquakes rather than common tremors, we propose adapting the probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) framework. The analogous solution involves modeling the likelihood and potential consequence ('magnitude') of different seismic events over time. Adapted to cybersecurity, this means developing a model to quantify the potential impact severity (e.g., financial loss, operational downtime, reputational damage) and the estimated frequency of various breach types, from common phishing to rare but catastrophic infrastructure attacks. The resulting research would produce risk curves to identify which low-frequency, high-severity breach types pose the most significant threat, thereby informing a more nuanced and resilient defense strategy that moves beyond frequency-based prioritization.
